subject: Math
description: Counting could range from 1 through 20 with simple pictures, diagrams, models, or representations. Skip counting could be by 2s or 5s through 20 and could include finding a missing number or extending a pattern.

description: Times could be in one minute increments in a.m. or p.m. and could include up to +/- 24 hours or multiple days of elapsed time. Contexts will relate the time to an appropriate activity.
description_plain: Your child can understand and work with time in minutes using a.m. and p.m., including calculating elapsed time up to 24 hours or several days, related to everyday activities.

subject: Math
description: Times could be in one minute increments in a.m. or p.m. and could include up to +/- 24 hours or multiple days of elapsed time. Contexts will relate the time to an appropriate activity.
description_plain: Your child can understand and work with time in minutes using a.m. and p.m., including calculating elapsed time up to 24 hours or several days, related to everyday activities.

subject: Math
description: Tell time and measure elapsed time to the minute using analog and digital clocks, including with context.
description_plain: Your child can tell time and figure out how much time has passed to the minute using both analog and digital clocks, including in real-life situations.

subject: Math
description: Tell time and measure elapsed time to the minute using analog and digital clocks, including with context.
description_plain: Your child can tell time and figure out how much time has passed to the minute using both analog and digital clocks, including in real-life situations.

subject: Math
description: Tell time and measure elapsed time to the minute using analog and digital clocks, including with context.
description_plain: Your child can tell time and figure out how much time has passed to the minute using both analog and digital clocks, including in real-life situations.

subject: Math
description: Tell time and measure elapsed time to the minute using analog and digital clocks, including with context.
description_plain: Your child can tell time and figure out how much time has passed to the minute using both analog and digital clocks, including in real-life situations.

subject: Math
description: The description and equation could include addition and subtraction from 0 through 50 or multiplication or division from 0 through 40.
description_plain: Your child can understand and write equations from word problems using addition and subtraction with numbers up to 50, or multiplication and division with numbers up to 40.

subject: Math
description: The description and equation could include addition and subtraction from 0 through 50 or multiplication or division from 0 through 40.
description_plain: Your child can understand and write equations from word problems using addition and subtraction with numbers up to 50, or multiplication and division with numbers up to 40.

subject: Math
description: The description and equation could include addition and subtraction from 0 through 50 or multiplication or division from 0 through 40.
description_plain: Your child can understand and write equations from word problems using addition and subtraction with numbers up to 50, or multiplication and division with numbers up to 40.

subject: Math
description: The description and equation could include addition and subtraction from 0 through 50 or multiplication or division from 0 through 40.
description_plain: Your child can understand and write equations from word problems using addition and subtraction with numbers up to 50, or multiplication and division with numbers up to 40.

subject: Math
description: Identify an equation when provided a verbal description in real world applications.
description_plain: Your child can recognize an equation when given a real-world description.

subject: Math
description: Identify an equation when provided a verbal description in real world applications.
description_plain: Your child can recognize an equation when given a real-world description.

subject: Math
description: Identify an equation when provided a verbal description in real world applications.
description_plain: Your child can recognize an equation when given a real-world description.

subject: Math
description: Identify an equation when provided a verbal description in real world applications.
description_plain: Your child can recognize an equation when given a real-world description.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing physical properties of common objects including size and shape to • recognizing additional physical properties including hardness/softness and weight/mass of common objects (e.g., a rock is harder than an egg; a balloon weighs less than a basketball) to • recognizing additional physical properties including volume and other representations of matter as a solid, liquid, and a gas/vapor.
description_plain: Your child can use simple pictures or diagrams to notice and compare things like size, shape, hardness, weight, and whether matter is a solid, liquid, or gas.

subject: Science
description: Recognize and compare the physical properties of matter in different phases.
description_plain: Your child understands and compares the physical properties of matter in solids, liquids, and gases.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing two solids mixed that do not form a new substance (e.g., rocks and soil, objects in sand, trail mix, marbles in playdough) to • recognizing solid and liquid mixtures that do not form a new substance (sand and water) to • identifying more complex mixtures that may form a new substance (e.g., one solid and one liquid - salt water, fruit punch; two liquids - paint, lemonade; two gases - air in a balloon, air in the atmosphere; one liquid and one gas – carbonation in soda, air bubbles in water).
description_plain: Your child can use pictures or diagrams to recognize when different solids or liquids are mixed and whether they form new substances or not.

subject: Science
description: Recognize when substances are mixed.
description_plain: Your child understands when substances are mixed together.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing the smaller parts of large common objects (e.g., cars, trucks, buses - wheels; houses - doors and windows; building blocks - smaller blocks; computers and tablets - screen and keyboard) to • recognizing the parts of common living organisms (e.g., dogs, cats, birds - legs, eyes, ears, wings; plants, trees - leaves, flowers, trunk) to • identifying more complex parts of common objects, living organisms, and Earth systems (e.g., atmosphere – clouds, fog; solar system – planets, moons, comets) including parts that are very small.
description_plain: Your child can use pictures or diagrams to identify smaller parts of objects, animals, plants, and Earth systems that can be seen without a microscope.

subject: Science
description: Recognize that objects, animals, and plants are made of smaller parts and identify various parts visible to the naked eye.
description_plain: Your child understands that objects, animals, and plants are made of smaller parts and can name parts they can see.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing objects that make light without referring to the concept or term “light” to • recognizing objects that make or produce light from those that do not while incorporating and referring to the concept and term “light” to • identifying basic forms of light based on common everyday uses or sources and incorporating the term “energy.”
description_plain: Your child can use pictures or diagrams to recognize things that make light and understand light as a form of energy from everyday sources.

subject: Science
description: Recognize light as a form of energy with everyday uses, applications, and sources.
description_plain: Your child understands that light is a type of energy and knows common ways we use and see light.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing objects that make noise or sound without referring to the concept or term “sound” to • recognizing objects that make or produce sound from those that do not while incorporating and referring to the concept and term “sound” to • identifying basic forms of sound based on common everyday uses or sources and incorporating the term “energy.”
description_plain: Your child can use pictures or diagrams to recognize things that make sound and understand sound as a form of energy from everyday sources.

subject: Science
description: Recognize sound as a form of energy with everyday uses, applications, and sources.
description_plain: Your child understands that sound is a type of energy and knows common ways we hear and use sound.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing objects that require electricity as compared to common unrelated objects that do not without referring to the term “electricity” to • recognizing objects that require electricity as compared to common unrelated objects that do not while incorporating the concept and term “electricity” to • identifying basic forms of electricity based on common everyday uses or sources and incorporating the term “energy.”
description_plain: Your child can use simple pictures or diagrams to tell which objects need electricity to work and can name electricity and energy when talking about these objects.

subject: Science
description: Recognize electricity as a form of energy with everyday uses, applications, and sources.
description_plain: Your child understands that electricity is a type of energy and can give examples of how we use it every day.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing objects that are at rest or in motion while using common terminology (e.g., sitting, still, moving) to • recognizing objects that are at rest or in motion by incorporating the concept of force and common terminology (e.g., push, pull) to • identifying objects that are at rest or in motion by incorporating use of the term “force” and changes in motion (e.g., direction, from motion to rest, from rest to motion) through common examples.
description_plain: Your child can look at pictures or diagrams and tell if something is moving or still, explain how pushes or pulls (forces) cause movement or stop it, and describe changes in motion.

subject: Science
description: Recognize objects in motion and changes in motion due to force.
description_plain: Your child understands that objects move or stop moving because of forces like pushes and pulls.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing activities that harm Earth to • recognizing simple and common choices that help protect the environment or conserve natural resources (e.g., picking up trash, recycling materials, turning off lights) as compared to common unrelated activities (e.g., playing outside, eating a meal) to • identifying simple and common choices that help protect the environment or conserve natural resources as compared to common activities that harm or pollute Earth (e.g., pollution from a factory, littering in streams or oceans).
description_plain: Your child can use pictures or diagrams to recognize actions that hurt the Earth and simple choices like recycling or turning off lights that help protect the environment.

subject: Science
description: Recognize ways in which people and communities protect Earth's environment and conserve natural resources.
description_plain: Your child understands how people and communities take care of Earth and save natural resources.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing common non-living features of Earth (e.g., lakes, rivers, streams, and oceans; rocks, mountains, volcanoes, and canyons; air and clouds) to • recognizing simple interactions among non-living and living features within common systems (e.g., clouds providing water/rain to lakes, rivers, and oceans; lakes, rivers, and oceans providing water to humans, plants, and animals) to • identifying processes that lead to erosion, weathering, and deposition linked to non-living features (e.g., mountains, rivers, streams, volcanoes).
description_plain: Your child can identify common non-living parts of Earth like lakes, mountains, and air, explain how these parts interact with living things, and describe how processes like erosion change the land.

subject: Science
description: Recognize common features of Earth's systems, simple interactions between those features, and the processes that shape Earth.
description_plain: Your child understands Earth's features, how they work together, and how natural processes shape the planet.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing living and non-living natural resources used in everyday life as compared to other unrelated items or objects to • distinguishing between living and non-living natural resources that are important to Virginia to • connecting living and non-living natural resources to their common use and where they come from (e.g., wood is burned for fire; sources of drinking water; paper comes from trees or forests).
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between living and non-living natural resources, know which ones are important in Virginia, and explain how we use them in daily life.

subject: Science
description: Recognize natural resources, including those important in Virginia, in connection with their common use and origin.
description_plain: Your child understands natural resources, especially those important in Virginia, and knows where they come from and how we use them.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing the difference between day and night (e.g., daylight and the sun versus darkness, the moon, and stars) to • recognizing that the sun provides the vast majority of light and heat energy to Earth (compared to the moon and other objects in the solar system) to • understanding that the sun gives light and heat energy to Earth and its organisms and influences the four major seasons.
description_plain: Your child can recognize the difference between day and night, know that the sun gives Earth most of its light and heat, and understand how the sun affects the seasons.

subject: Science
description: Recognize that the sun provides Earth with light and energy.
description_plain: Your child understands that the sun provides Earth with light and energy.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing Earth and the moon, including its phases, compared to other common, unrelated objects on Earth to • recognizing Earth, the sun, and the moon using their relative sizes and positions to • understanding the concept and terminology of orbit and revolution.
description_plain: Your child can identify Earth and the moon, notice the moon’s phases, compare their sizes and positions with the sun, and understand that they move in orbits.

subject: Science
description: Recognize the relationships between Earth, the moon, and the sun.
description_plain: Your child understands how Earth, the moon, and the sun are related and how they move around each other.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing the sun and Earth as compared to common unrelated objects on Earth to • recognizing the sun and Earth as compared to other objects in the solar system to • comparing simple physical characteristics (e.g., size, shape) of objects in the solar system.
description_plain: Your child can recognize the sun and Earth compared to other objects on Earth and in space, and compare their size and shape to other solar system objects.

subject: Science
description: Recognize and compare objects in the solar system and their features.
description_plain: Your child understands and can compare objects in the solar system and their features.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing simple weather conditions (rainy, cloudy, sunny, foggy, thunder and lightning) to • connecting physical conditions to weather conditions (e.g., wet to rain, dry or hot to sunny, lightning to thunderstorm) to • identifying more complex storm conditions (e.g., hurricane, tornado, blizzard) and their physical conditions.
description_plain: Your child can recognize different weather types like rain, sun, and storms, connect weather to physical signs like wetness or lightning, and identify bigger storms like hurricanes or tornadoes.

subject: Science
description: Recognize different types of weather conditions and their characteristics.
description_plain: Your child understands different kinds of weather and what makes them happen.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing oceans as compared to common non-water objects or features (e.g., rocks, mountains, forests) to • recognizing oceans as compared to other common water features (e.g., lakes, rivers, streams) to • identifying common organisms that live in oceans.
description_plain: Your child can tell what oceans are by comparing them to land features and other water bodies, and can name common animals that live in oceans.

subject: Science
description: Recognize oceans and identify the organisms that live in them.
description_plain: Your child understands what oceans are and can identify some of the creatures that live there.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: recognizing living and non-living parts of an environment or ecosystem to, recognizing interactions between living and non-living parts (e.g., water, habitat, shelter) of an ecosystem to, identifying simple interactions between living organisms or among groups of living organisms (e.g., predator-prey, competitive, mutually beneficial).
description_plain: Your child can use simple pictures to show the living and non-living parts of an environment and explain how they interact, like how animals and water or shelter affect each other.

subject: Science
description: Recognize ways in which living organisms interact with other living organisms and non-living parts of an ecosystem.
description_plain: Your child understands how living things interact with other living things and with non-living parts of their environment.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: recognizing animals and plants using common terminology to, recognizing that animals need food, air, and water and that plants use soil, air, water, and light to, identifying and connecting unique structures of plants and animals that help them obtain what they need to grow and survive.
description_plain: Your child can use simple pictures to name animals and plants, explain what they need to live, and identify body parts that help them survive.

subject: Science
description: Recognize that living organisms have unique structures that help them obtain what they need to grow and survive.
description_plain: Your child understands that animals and plants have special body parts that help them get what they need to grow and live.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: recognizing plants (e.g., plants, trees, and flowers) need light, air, and water to grow to, identifying simple parts (e.g., roots, stems, leaves, flower, fruit) of plants that help them get light, air, and water to, comparing growth of plants when given appropriate or inappropriate amounts of light, air, and water.
description_plain: Your child can use simple pictures to show that plants need light, air, and water to grow and can name parts of plants that help them get these things.

subject: Science
description: Recognize that plants need light, air, and water to grow.
description_plain: Your child understands that plants need light, air, and water to grow.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing common objects in relation to their weight and mass to • connecting mass, force, and motion in common situations, (e.g., pushing/pulling objects) to • comparing force, mass, and change in motion (e.g., more mass requires more force to move or more force moves the same mass more quickly) in common situations (e.g., pushing an object uphill, throwing/kicking/hitting a ball harder).
description_plain: Your child can use pictures to explain how the weight of objects, the force used to move them, and their motion are connected, like pushing a heavy box or kicking a ball.

subject: Science
description: Recognize that the force, mass, and motion of objects are related and comparable.
description_plain: Your child understands that how hard you push, how heavy something is, and how it moves are all related.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing objects that are at rest or in motion while using common terminology (e.g., sitting, still, moving) to • recognizing objects that are at rest or in motion by incorporating the concepts of forces (force, push, pull) and changes in motion (e.g., change in direction, motion to rest, rest to motion) to • identifying and comparing simple actions and reactions for common objects at rest or in motion (i.e., more or less force, presence or absence of force).
description_plain: Your child can use pictures to recognize when things are still or moving, explain how forces like pushes and pulls change motion, and compare actions and reactions.

subject: Science
description: Recognize objects in motion involving actions and reactions.
description_plain: Your child understands that objects move or stay still because of actions and reactions involving forces.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing basic forms/types of energy compared to other forms/types of energy (i.e., kinetic, electrical, sound, thermal, and light energy) to • identifying basic forms/types of energy based on common use or source to • understanding that energy is transferred and transformed to help people meet their needs (e.g., electrical energy lights a light bulb and heats a stove).
description_plain: Your child can use pictures to identify different types of energy like light, heat, and sound, and explain how energy changes form to help us, like electricity lighting a bulb.

subject: Science
description: Recognize basic forms of energy and that energy is transferred and transformed.
description_plain: Your child understands basic types of energy and knows that energy can change from one form to another.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing physical properties of common objects including size and shape to • recognizing and comparing simple physical/chemical properties of common objects (e.g., size, shape, hardness/softness, weight, mass, and density) to • identifying changes in physical/chemical properties that result from common activities (e.g., cooking an egg, dissolving sugar in water, boiling water, burning wood).
description_plain: Your child can use pictures to describe things like size, shape, and hardness, and notice how these change when things are cooked, melted, or burned.

subject: Science
description: Recognize and measure the physical and chemical properties of matter including before or after a physical or chemical change occurs.
description_plain: Your child understands how to observe and measure the properties of matter and notice changes after physical or chemical reactions.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing the smaller parts of large common objects (e.g., cars, trucks, buses, bikes, scooter - wheels; houses and other building - doors and windows; building blocks - smaller blocks; smart phones, computers, laptops, and tablets – screen, keyboard) to • recognizing smaller and more complex parts of common objects and living organisms to • identifying more complex parts of common objects and living organisms including those that are very small or too small to be seen with the naked eye (e.g., objects, water, animals, plants - atoms and molecules) including that we can use technology to see them (e.g., magnifying glass, microscope).
description_plain: Your child can use pictures to recognize that objects, animals, and plants are made of smaller parts, some too small to see without tools like microscopes.

subject: Science
description: Recognize that objects, animals, and plants are made of smaller parts and identify various seen and unseen parts.
description_plain: Your child understands that everything is made of smaller parts, including tiny parts we need special tools to see.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing the three phases of water to • connecting the phases of water to common experiences at different points in the water cycle (e.g., liquid water falling as rain, solid water falling as snow or hail, water vapor rising from a boiling pot of water) to • identifying a specific phase of water as compared to other phases of water using common examples at different points in the water cycle (e.g., ice melts to a liquid, a puddle of water evaporates to a gas, rain falls as a liquid from the atmosphere).
description_plain: Your child can use pictures to identify water as a solid, liquid, or gas and explain how water changes between these forms during the water cycle.

subject: Science
description: Recognize water phases and how water changes its phase through the water cycle.
description_plain: Your child understands the different forms of water and how water changes from ice to liquid to vapor in the water cycle.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing the difference between hot and cold in common contexts to • recognizing that hot and cold are related to measures of temperature or changes in temperature to • recognizing common examples of heat transfer and how it can be minimized (e.g., wearing a coat to stay warm, using an oven mitt) or maximized (e.g., sitting in the sun to get warm, using a fan to get cool).
description_plain: Your child can use pictures to explain what hot and cold mean, how temperature measures heat, and how heat moves, like wearing a coat to stay warm.

subject: Science
description: Recognize temperature as a measure of how hot or cold matter is and that thermal energy is transferable.
description_plain: Your child understands that temperature shows how hot or cold something is and that heat can move from one thing to another.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing different types of common objects or materials that come from Earth to • recognizing different natural resources and incorporating the term “natural resource” to • connecting renewable or nonrenewable natural resource with common uses and objects (e.g., paper from forests/trees/wood, energy from coal/sun, drinking water from watersheds).
description_plain: Your child can use simple pictures to identify natural resources from Earth and explain how things like paper, energy, and water come from these resources.

subject: Science
description: Recognize different materials humans use that come from Earth's natural resources.
description_plain: Your child understands that many materials people use come from Earth's natural resources.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing activities that pollute or harm Earth (e.g., automobile with exhaust, trash on the ground) compared to unrelated and/or common activities that help protect Earth (e.g., walking, riding bike, picking up trash) to • recognizing simple and common options that help protect the environment or conserve natural resources (e.g., picking up trash, recycling materials, turning off lights) to • identifying ways to protect Earth’s environment or conserve natural resources as compared to activities that pollute or harm Earth’s environment (e.g., pollution from a factory, plastic in streams or oceans).
description_plain: Your child can use pictures to tell which activities harm the Earth and which ones help protect it, like recycling or picking up trash.

subject: Science
description: Recognize ways in which people and communities use and impact Earth's environment and resources.
description_plain: Your child understands how people and communities use Earth's resources and how their actions affect the environment.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing simple weather conditions (e.g., rainy, cloudy, sunny, foggy, thunder and lightning) to • recognizing that changes in simple weather conditions are connected to decisions about everyday activities or actions to • connecting weather to characteristics of the atmosphere (e.g., temperature, cloudy versus clear, windy versus calm) and common weather tools and information (e.g., thermometers, weather forecasts).
description_plain: Your child can recognize different weather types and explain how weather affects daily choices using simple pictures and tools like thermometers.

subject: Science
description: Recognize different types of weather conditions and their characteristics.
description_plain: Your child understands different kinds of weather and what makes them happen.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing the difference between day and night to • recognizing that the sun gives the vast majority of light and heat energy to Earth and its organisms to • recognizing the connection between Earth's rotation and daytime and nighttime and Earth's tilt on its axis and the four major seasons.
description_plain: Your child can use pictures to explain day and night, how the sun gives Earth light and heat, and how Earth's movement causes seasons.

subject: Science
description: Recognize that the sun provides Earth with light and energy.
description_plain: Your child understands that the sun provides Earth with light and energy.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing the direction that common objects fall due to gravity on Earth to • recognizing the role of gravity in the movement of Earth and the sun or Earth and the moon to • recognizing the role of gravity in the movement of other objects in the solar system (e.g., planets and the sun, moons of other planets, comets and the sun).
description_plain: Your child can use pictures to show how gravity makes things fall and affects the movement of Earth, the sun, moon, and other space objects.

subject: Science
description: Recognize that gravity influences the way objects move on Earth and in space.
description_plain: Your child understands that gravity controls how objects move on Earth and in space.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing the sun, Earth, and moon as compared to everyday objects on Earth to • recognizing the sun, Earth, and moon as compared to other related objects in the solar system to • comparing characteristics (e.g., size, shape, position, composition) of various space objects (e.g., sun, Earth, moon, planets, comets, asteroids) in the solar system.
description_plain: Your child can compare the sun, Earth, moon, and other space objects by their size, shape, and position using simple pictures.

subject: Science
description: Recognize and compare objects in the solar system and their features.
description_plain: Your child understands and compares objects in the solar system and what makes them different.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing highly similar animals based on their physical characteristics (e.g., dogs with other dogs, birds with other birds) to • recognizing similar animals based on broader physical characteristics (e.g., wolf with dog, lion with cat) to • recognizing fossils of common extinct organisms are similar to organisms living today (e.g., fern fossil similar to today’s plants, dinosaur fossil similar to today’s lizards)
description_plain: Your child can recognize animals that look alike and see how fossils of extinct animals are similar to animals living today.

subject: Science
description: Recognize anatomically similar organisms.
description_plain: Your child understands that some animals have similar body parts and features.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing that the offspring of a living organism (plants, animals, humans) are the same species of living organism to • recognizing the offspring of a living organism (plants, animals, humans) may not be identical and may have similar traits with variations.
description_plain: Your child knows that babies of plants and animals are the same kind but may look a little different from their parents.

subject: Science
description: Recognize that reproduction produces offspring with similar though varied traits.
description_plain: Your child understands that reproduction makes offspring that share traits with their parents but also have differences.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing individual living organisms and groups of living organisms to • recognizing various resources that living organisms need to grow and sustain their population to • recognizing that simple changes in resources, including those due to human activity, might affect an individual living organism or groups of living organisms or their population.
description_plain: Your child can identify living things and the resources they need, and explain how changes in these resources affect their growth.

subject: Science
description: Recognize living organisms in an ecosystem, the resources available in that ecosystem, and how changes in resources (i.e., food, water, shelter, habitat) affect the growth of their population.
description_plain: Your child understands that living things in an ecosystem need food, water, and shelter, and that changes in these affect their populations.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing simple traits of living organisms that help them survive (e.g., a rabbit’s ears help it hear predators) to • recognizing traits that help one animal survive as compared to unrelated traits of other animals to • identifying the function of traits that helps individual animals or groups of the same animal adapt or survive (e.g., a giraffe’s long neck helps it reach food from the tops of trees, a tiger’s stripes help it hide in tall grass).
description_plain: Your child can recognize traits in animals that help them survive, like a giraffe’s long neck or a tiger’s stripes.

subject: Science
description: Recognize traits that help living organisms adapt and survive.
description_plain: Your child understands that certain traits help living things adapt and survive in their environment.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing living and non-living parts of an environment or ecosystem to • identifying interactions between living and non-living parts (e.g., habitat, shelter, water) of an ecosystem to • identifying and understanding more complex interactions between living organisms or among groups of living organisms (e.g., predator-prey, competitive, mutually beneficial) including through simple food chains/webs.
description_plain: Your child can use simple pictures or diagrams to identify living and non-living parts of an environment and explain how they interact, including food chains and relationships like predator-prey.

subject: Science
description: Recognize ways in which living organisms interact with other living organisms and non-living parts of an ecosystem.
description_plain: Your child understands how living things interact with other living things and with non-living parts of their environment.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing that living things need food to • recognizing food is used for “energy” by living things to grow and survive to • recognizing the amount of energy or expected growth varies with the amount or type/quality of food.
description_plain: Your child can use simple pictures or diagrams to show that living things need food for energy and growth, and that the amount or type of food affects how much they grow.

subject: Science
description: Recognize that living organisms need food to obtain energy and grow.
description_plain: Your child understands that living things need food to get energy and grow.

subject: Science
description: Using simple pictures, diagrams, or representations, concepts could range from: • recognizing that plants need light, air, and water to survive and grow to • recognizing materials or conditions that would not help them grow (e.g., complete darkness, no water, salt) to • recognizing the term and role of photosynthesis and characterizing or comparing the growth of a plant, tree, or flower when different amounts of light, air, or water are provided.
description_plain: Your child can use simple pictures or diagrams to show that plants need light, air, and water to grow, understand what hurts their growth, and learn about photosynthesis.

subject: Science
description: Recognize that plants need light, air, and water to grow through a process called photosynthesis.
description_plain: Your child understands that plants need light, air, and water to grow through a process called photosynthesis.

subject: Science
