description: volumes of two containers using the terms more or less; and
description_plain: Your child can compare how much two containers hold by saying which has more or less.

subject: Math
description: weights of two objects using the terms heavier or lighter;
description_plain: Your child can compare two objects by weight and say which is heavier or lighter.

subject: Math
description: heights of two objects using the terms taller or shorter;
description_plain: Your child can compare two objects by height and say which is taller or shorter.

subject: Math
description: lengths of two objects using the terms longer or shorter;
description_plain: Your child can compare two objects by length and say which is longer or shorter.

subject: Math
description: Use direct comparisons to compare, describe, and justify the:
description_plain: Your child can compare two things directly and explain which is longer, taller, heavier, holds more, or takes more time.

subject: Math
description: The student will reason mathematically by making direct comparisons between two objects or events using the attributes of length, height, weight, volume, and time.
description_plain: Your child can compare two objects or events by looking at their length, height, weight, volume, or time.

subject: Math
description: Model and solve single-step contextual problems (join, separate, and part-part-whole) using 10 or fewer concrete objects.
description_plain: Your child can use up to 10 objects to solve simple problems like joining groups, taking some away, or splitting into parts.

subject: Math
description: Model and identify the number that makes 10 when added to a given number less than or equal to 10.
description_plain: Your child can find the number that adds to another number to make 10, using numbers 10 or less.

subject: Math
description: Use objects, drawings, words, or numbers to compose and decompose numbers less than or equal to 10 in multiple ways.
description_plain: Your child can use objects, drawings, words, or numbers to break apart and put together numbers up to 10 in different ways.

subject: Math
description: Model and identify the number that makes 5 when added to a given number less than or equal to 5.
description_plain: Your child can find the number that adds to a given number (up to 5) to make 5.

subject: Math
description: Recognize and describe with fluency part-part-whole relationships for numbers up to 5 in a variety of configurations.
description_plain: Your child understands how numbers up to 5 can be split into parts and put back together in different ways.

subject: Math
description: Use objects, drawings, words, or numbers to compose and decompose numbers less than or equal to 5 in multiple ways.
description_plain: Your child can use objects, drawings, words, or numbers to break apart and put together numbers up to 5 in different ways.

subject: Math
description: The student will model and solve single-step contextual problems using addition and subtraction with whole numbers within 10.
description_plain: Your child can solve simple addition and subtraction problems with numbers up to 10 using real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Compare numbers up to 30, to the benchmarks of 5 and 10 using various models (e.g., five frames, ten frames, number paths [a prelude to number lines], beaded racks, hands) using the terms greater than, less than, or the same as (equal to).
description_plain: Your child can compare numbers up to 30 to 5 and 10 using tools like number frames or fingers and say if they are bigger, smaller, or the same.

subject: Math
description: Compare two sets containing up to 30 concrete objects or pictorial models, using the terms more, fewer, or the same as (equal to).
description_plain: Your child can compare two groups of up to 30 objects and say which has more, fewer, or if they are the same.

subject: Math
description: Given a numeral up to 30, construct a set which has more, fewer, or the same number of objects using concrete or pictorial models.
description_plain: Your child can make a group with more, fewer, or the same number of objects as a given number up to 30.

subject: Math
description: Given a set of up to 30 objects, construct another set which has more, fewer, or the same number of objects using concrete or pictorial models.
description_plain: Your child can make a new group with more, fewer, or the same number of objects as another group of up to 30 objects.

subject: Math
description: Determine and write the numeral that corresponds to the total number of objects in a given set of 30 or fewer concrete objects or pictorial models.
description_plain: Your child can count a group of up to 30 objects and write the matching number.

subject: Math
description: Construct a set of objects that corresponds to a given numeral within 30, including an empty set.
description_plain: Your child can make a group of objects that matches a given number up to 30, including zero.

subject: Math
description: Read, write, and identify the numerals 0 through 30.
description_plain: Your child can read, write, and recognize numbers from 0 to 30.

subject: Math
description: The student will identify, represent, and compare quantities up to 30.
description_plain: Your child can recognize, show, and compare numbers up to 30.

subject: Math
description: Group a collection of up to 100 objects (e.g., counters, pennies, cubes) into sets of ten and count by tens to determine the total (e.g., there are 3 groups of ten and 6 leftovers, 36 total objects).
description_plain: Your child can group up to 100 objects into sets of ten and count by tens to find the total number.

subject: Math
description: Use objects, drawings, words, or numbers to compose and decompose numbers 11-19 into a ten and some ones.
description_plain: Your child can use objects, drawings, words, or numbers to show numbers 11 to 19 as one group of ten and some extra ones.

subject: Math
description: State the number before, without counting, when given any number between 1 and 20.
description_plain: Your child can say the number that comes before any number from 1 to 20 without counting.

subject: Math
description: State the number after, without counting, when given any number between 0 and 30.
description_plain: Your child can say the number that comes after any number from 0 to 30 without counting.

subject: Math
description: Count backward orally by ones when given any number between 1 and 20.
description_plain: Your child can count backward by ones starting from any number between 1 and 20.

subject: Math
description: Count forward orally by ones, within 100, starting at any given number.
description_plain: Your child can count forward by ones up to 100, starting at any number.

subject: Math
description: Count forward orally by ones from 0 to 100.
description_plain: Your child can count forward by ones from 0 to 100.

subject: Math
description: Represent forward counting by ones using a variety of tools, including five-frames, tenframes, and number paths (a prelude to number lines).
description_plain: Your child can count forward by ones using tools like five-frames, ten-frames, and number paths.

subject: Math
description: Recognize and explain that the number of objects remains the same regardless of the arrangement or the order in which the objects are counted.
description_plain: Your child understands that the number of objects stays the same no matter how they are arranged or counted.

subject: Math
description: Use one-to-one correspondence to determine how many are in a given set containing 30 or fewer concrete objects (e.g., cubes, pennies, balls), and describe the last number named as the total number of objects counted.
description_plain: Your child can count up to 30 objects one by one and say the last number as the total amount.

subject: Math
description: The student will utilize flexible counting strategies to determine and describe quantities up to 100.
description_plain: Your child uses different ways to count and describe amounts up to 100.

subject: Math
description: Transfer a repeating or increasing pattern from one form to another.
description_plain: Your child can copy a repeating or growing pattern from one form to another.

subject: Math
description: Create a repeating or increasing pattern using objects, pictures, movements, colors, or geometric figures.
description_plain: Your child can make repeating or growing patterns using objects, pictures, movements, colors, or shapes.

subject: Math
description: Analyze a repeating or increasing pattern and generalize the change to extend the pattern using objects, colors, movements, pictures, or geometric figures.
description_plain: Your child can look at a repeating or growing pattern and figure out how it changes to continue the pattern.

subject: Math
description: Identify and describe repeating and increasing patterns.
description_plain: Your child can find and talk about repeating and growing patterns.

subject: Math
description: The student will identify, describe, extend, create, and transfer repeating patterns and increasing patterns using various representations.
description_plain: Your child can find, describe, continue, create, and copy repeating and growing patterns in different ways.

subject: Math
description: draw conclusions about the data and make predictions based on the data.
description_plain: Your child can make guesses and conclusions based on information from data.

subject: Math
description: ask and answer questions about the data represented in object graphs, picture graphs, and tables (e.g., total number of data points represented, how many in each category, how many more or less are in one category than another); and
description_plain: Your child can ask and answer questions about data shown in object graphs, picture graphs, and tables.

subject: Math
description: Analyze data represented in object graphs, picture graphs, and tables and communicate results:
description_plain: Your child can look at data in graphs and tables and explain what it shows.

subject: Math
description: Represent a data set (vertically or horizontally) using object graphs, picture graphs, and tables.
description_plain: Your child can show data using object graphs, picture graphs, or tables.

subject: Math
description: Organize and represent a data set by sorting the collected data using various methods (e.g., tallying, T-charts).
description_plain: Your child can organize data by sorting it using ways like tally marks or T-charts.

subject: Math
description: Determine the data needed to answer a posed question and collect the data using various methods (e.g., counting objects, drawing pictures, tallying).
description_plain: Your child can decide what data is needed to answer a question and collect it by counting, drawing, or tallying.

subject: Math
description: Pose questions, given a predetermined context, that require the collection of data (limited to 25 or fewer data points for no more than four categories).
description_plain: Your child can ask questions that need data collection with up to 25 items in no more than four groups.

subject: Math
description: Describe and label attributes of a set of objects that has been sorted.
description_plain: Your child can describe and name features of a group of objects that have been sorted.

subject: Math
description: Sort and classify concrete objects into appropriate subsets (categories) based on one or two attributes, such as size, shape, color, and/or thickness (e.g., sort a set of objects that are both red and thick).
description_plain: Your child can sort objects into groups based on one or two features like size, shape, color, or thickness.

subject: Math
description: The student will apply the data cycle (pose questions; collect or acquire data; organize and represent data; and analyze data and communicate results) with a focus on object graphs, picture graphs, and tables.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions, collect data, organize it, and explain what the data shows using graphs and tables.

subject: Math
description: Determine the day/date before and after a given day/date (e.g., Today is the 8 th , so yesterday was the ?), and a date that is a specific number of days/weeks in the past or future (e.g., Tim’s birthday is in 10 days, what will be the date of his birthday?).
description_plain: Your child can figure out the day or date before or after a given day and find a date a certain number of days or weeks away.

subject: Math
description: Use ordinal numbers first through tenth to describe the relative position of specific days/dates (e.g., What is the first Monday in October? What day of the week is May 6th?).
description_plain: Your child can use words like first, second, and third to talk about the order of days and dates, such as finding the first Monday in a month.

subject: Math
description: Identify specific days/dates on a calendar (e.g., What date is Saturday? How many Fridays are in October?).
description_plain: Your child can find specific days and dates on a calendar and count how many times a certain day happens in a month.

subject: Math
description: Match the time shown on a digital clock to an analog clock to the hour and half-hour.
description_plain: Your child can match the time shown on a digital clock to the same time on an analog clock for hours and half-hours.

subject: Math
description: Describe the location of the minute hand relative to time to the hour and half-hour on an analog clock.
description_plain: Your child can explain where the minute hand points on a clock when telling time to the hour and half-hour.

subject: Math
description: Describe the location of the hour hand relative to time to the hour and half-hour on an analog clock.
description_plain: Your child can explain where the hour hand points on a clock when telling time to the hour and half-hour.

subject: Math
description: Tell time to the hour and half-hour, using analog and digital clocks.
description_plain: Your child can tell time to the hour and half-hour using both analog and digital clocks.

subject: Math
description: Describe the units of time represented on a clock as minutes and hours.
description_plain: Your child understands that clocks show time using minutes and hours.

subject: Math
description: Identify different tools to measure time including clocks (analog and digital) and calendar.
description_plain: Your child can recognize different tools that show time, like analog clocks, digital clocks, and calendars.

subject: Math
description: The student will demonstrate an understanding of the concept of passage of time (to the nearest hour and half-hour) and the calendar.
description_plain: Your child understands how time passes by hours and half-hours and can use a calendar to track days.

subject: Math
description: Compose larger plane figures by combining two or three simple plane figures (triangles, squares, and/or rectangles).
description_plain: Your child can put together two or three simple shapes like triangles, squares, or rectangles to make bigger shapes.

subject: Math
description: Recognize and name the angles found in rectangles and squares as right angles.
description_plain: Your child can find and name right angles in squares and rectangles.

subject: Math
description: Identify, name, and describe representations of circles, squares, rectangles, and triangles, regardless of orientation, in different environments and explain reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can recognize and name circles, squares, rectangles, and triangles no matter how they are turned and explain why.

subject: Math
description: Draw and name the plane figure (circle, square, rectangle, triangle) when given information about the number of sides, vertices, and angles.
description_plain: Your child can draw and name shapes like circles, squares, rectangles, and triangles when told how many sides, corners, and angles they have.

subject: Math
description: Sort plane figures based on their characteristics (e.g., number of sides, vertices, angles, curved).
description_plain: Your child can sort shapes by their features, like how many sides or corners they have or if they are curved.

subject: Math
description: Describe triangles, squares, and rectangles using the terms sides, vertices, and angles. Describe a circle using terms such as round and curved.
description_plain: Your child can describe triangles, squares, and rectangles by their sides, corners, and angles, and describe circles as round and curved.

subject: Math
description: The student will describe, sort, draw, and name plane figures (circles, triangles, squares, and rectangles), and compose larger plane figures by combining simple plane figures.
description_plain: Your child can describe, sort, draw, and name basic shapes and make bigger shapes by combining simple ones.

subject: Math
description: Measure the length, weight, or volume of the same object or container with two different units and describe how and why the measurements differ.
description_plain: Your child can measure the same object using two different units and explain how and why the measurements are different.

subject: Math
description: volumes of two containers and compare the measurements using the terms more, less, or the same as.
description_plain: Your child can compare how much two containers hold and say which one holds more, less, or the same.

subject: Math
description: weights of two objects (using a balance scale or a pan scale) and compare the measurements using the terms lighter, heavier, or the same as; and
description_plain: Your child can compare the weight of two objects using a scale and say which one is lighter, heavier, or if they weigh the same.

subject: Math
description: lengths of two objects (units laid end to end with no gaps or overlaps) and compare the measurements using the terms longer/shorter, taller/shorter, or the same as;
description_plain: Your child can measure and compare the length of two objects by lining units end to end and say which is longer, shorter, taller, or if they are the same.

subject: Math
description: Use nonstandard units to measure the:
description_plain: Your child can use things like blocks or paper clips to measure length, weight, or volume instead of standard tools.

subject: Math
description: The student will reason mathematically using nonstandard units to measure and compare objects by length, weight, and volume.
description_plain: Your child understands how to measure and compare how long, heavy, or full things are using everyday items as measuring tools.

subject: Math
description: Write an equation that could be used to represent the solution to an oral, written, or picture problem.
description_plain: Your child can write a math equation that matches a story problem or picture.

subject: Math
description: Use concrete materials to model an equation that represents the relationship of two expressions of equal value.
description_plain: Your child uses objects to show and understand that two math expressions have the same value.

subject: Math
description: Use concrete materials to model, identify, and justify when two expressions are not equal (e.g., 10 - 3 is not equal to 3 + 5).
description_plain: Your child uses objects to show and explain when two math expressions are not equal.

subject: Math
description: Describe the equal symbol (=) as a balance representing an equivalent relationship between expressions on either side of the equal symbol (e.g., 6 and 1 is the same as 4 and 3; 6 + 1 is balanced with 4 + 3; 6 + 1 = 4 + 3).
description_plain: Your child understands that the equal sign (=) means both sides have the same amount, like a balanced scale.

subject: Math
description: Identify and use (+) as a symbol for addition and (-) as a symbol for subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can recognize and use the plus (+) sign for adding and the minus (-) sign for subtracting.

subject: Math
description: Determine the unknown whole number that will result in a sum or difference of 10 or 20 (e.g., 14 - __ = 10 or 15 + __ = 20).
description_plain: Your child can find the missing number in addition or subtraction problems that add up to 10 or 20.

subject: Math
description: Represent, solve, and justify solutions to single-step addition and subtraction problems (join, separate, and part-part-whole) within 20, including those in context, using words, objects, drawings, or numbers.
description_plain: Your child can solve simple addition and subtraction problems within 20 using objects, drawings, or numbers and explain their answers.

subject: Math
description: Solve addition and subtraction problems within 20 using various strategies (e.g., inverse relationships: if 9 + 3 = 12 then 12 - 3 = 9; decomposition using known sums/differences: 9 + 7 can be thought of as 9 decomposed into 2 and 7, then use doubles, 7 + 7 = 14; 14 + 2 = 16 or decompose the 7 into 1 and 6; make a ten: 1 + 9 = 10; 10 + 6 = 16).
description_plain: Your child uses different ways to solve addition and subtraction problems within 20, like knowing that if 9 + 3 = 12 then 12 - 3 = 9.

subject: Math
description: Investigate, recognize, and describe part-part-whole relationships for numbers up to 20 in a variety of configurations (e.g., beaded racks, double ten frames).
description_plain: Your child explores and explains how numbers up to 20 can be split into parts in different ways.

subject: Math
description: Recall with automaticity addition and subtraction facts within 10.
description_plain: Your child quickly remembers addition and subtraction facts for numbers up to 10.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate fluency with addition and subtraction within 10 by applying reasoning strategies (e.g., count on/count back, one more/one less, doubles, make ten).
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract within 10 easily by using strategies like counting on, counting back, or making ten.

subject: Math
description: Recognize and describe with fluency part-part-whole relationships for numbers up to 10 in a variety of configurations.
description_plain: Your child understands and can explain how numbers up to 10 can be broken into parts in different ways.

subject: Math
description: The student will recall with automaticity addition and subtraction facts within 10 and represent, solve, and justify solutions to single-step problems, including those in context, using addition and subtraction with whole numbers within 20.
description_plain: Your child quickly recalls addition and subtraction facts within 10 and can solve and explain simple addition and subtraction problems up to 20.

subject: Math
description: Describe and justify how shares are equal pieces or equal parts of the whole (limited to halves, fourths) when given a contextual problem.
description_plain: Your child can explain that shares like halves and fourths are equal parts of a whole object or group.

subject: Math
description: Represent and name halves and fourths of a whole, using a region/area model (e.g., pie pieces, pattern blocks, paper folding, drawings) and a set model (e.g., eggs, marbles, counters) limited to two or four items.
description_plain: Your child can show and name halves and fourths using pictures, folding paper, or groups of objects like eggs or marbles.

subject: Math
description: Represent equal shares of a whole with two or four sharers, when given a contextual problem.
description_plain: Your child can divide a whole into two or four equal parts when given a real-life problem.

subject: Math
description: The student will use mathematical reasoning and justification to solve contextual problems that involve partitioning models into two and four equal-sized parts.
description_plain: Your child uses thinking and explanations to solve problems about dividing things into two or four equal parts.

subject: Math
description: Order three sets, each set containing up to 120 objects, from least to greatest, and greatest to least.
description_plain: Your child can put three groups of objects (up to 120 in each group) in order from smallest to largest and largest to smallest.

subject: Math
description: Compare two numbers between 0 and 120 represented pictorially or with concrete objects using the terms greater than, less than, or equal to.
description_plain: Your child can look at two numbers up to 120 shown with pictures or objects and say which number is bigger, smaller, or if they are the same.

subject: Math
description: Describe the number of groups of tens and ones when given a two-digit number and justify reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can explain how many groups of ten and how many ones are in a two-digit number and tell why.

subject: Math
description: Create a concrete or pictorial representation of a number using tens and ones and write the corresponding numeral up to 120 (e.g., 47 can be represented as 47 ones or it can be grouped into 4 tens with 7 ones left over).
description_plain: Your child can show a number up to 120 using groups of tens and ones with objects or drawings and write the matching number.

subject: Math
description: Estimate the number of objects (up to 120) in a given collection and justify the reasonableness of an answer.
description_plain: Your child can guess how many objects are in a group up to 120 and explain why their guess makes sense.

subject: Math
description: Read and write numerals 0-120 in sequence and out of sequence.
description_plain: Your child can read and write numbers from 0 to 120 in order and also in mixed-up order.

subject: Math
description: The student will represent, compare, and order quantities up to 120.
description_plain: Your child understands how to show, compare, and put in order numbers up to 120.

subject: Math
description: Count by ones, fives, or tens to determine the value of a collection of like coins (pennies, nickels, or dimes), whose total value is 100 cents or less.
description_plain: Your child can count by 1s, 5s, or 10s to find the total value of pennies, nickels, or dimes up to 100 cents.

subject: Math
description: Identify a penny, nickel, and dime by their attributes and describe the number of pennies equivalent to a nickel and a dime.
description_plain: Your child can recognize pennies, nickels, and dimes by how they look and say how many pennies equal a nickel or a dime.

subject: Math
description: Group a collection of up to 120 objects into tens and ones, and count to determine the total (e.g., 5 groups of ten and 6 ones is equal to 56 total objects).
description_plain: Your child can group up to 120 objects into tens and ones and count them to find the total number.

subject: Math
description: Represent forward counting patterns when counting by groups of 2 up to at least 30 using a variety of tools (e.g., beaded number strings, number paths [a prelude to number lines], 120 chart).
description_plain: Your child can count forward by twos up to 30 using tools like beads, number paths, or charts.

subject: Math
description: Represent forward counting patterns when counting by groups of 5 and groups of 10 up to 120 using a variety of tools (e.g., objects, coins, 120 chart).
description_plain: Your child can count forward by fives and tens up to 120 using objects, coins, or charts.

subject: Math
description: Count backward orally by ones when given any number between 1 and 30.
description_plain: Your child can count backward out loud by ones from any number between 1 and 30.

subject: Math
description: Count forward orally by ones from 0 to 120 starting at any number between 0 and 120.
description_plain: Your child can count forward out loud by ones from any number between 0 and 120.

subject: Math
description: The student will utilize flexible counting strategies to determine and describe quantities up to 120.
description_plain: Your child can use different ways to count and describe numbers up to 120.

subject: Math
description: Transfer a given repeating or increasing pattern from one form to another (e.g., objects, pictures, numbers) and explain the connection between the two patterns.
description_plain: Your child can copy a repeating or growing pattern from one form (like objects or pictures) to another and explain how they are connected.

subject: Math
description: Create a repeating or increasing pattern using various representations (e.g., objects, pictures, numbers).
description_plain: Your child can make a repeating or growing pattern using things like objects, pictures, or numbers.

subject: Math
description: Analyze a repeating or increasing pattern and generalize the change to extend the pattern using objects, pictures, and numbers.
description_plain: Your child can look at a repeating or growing pattern and figure out how it changes to keep the pattern going using objects, pictures, or numbers.

subject: Math
description: Identify and describe repeating and increasing patterns.
description_plain: Your child can find and talk about repeating and growing patterns.

subject: Math
description: The student will describe, extend, create, and transfer repeating and increasing patterns (limited to addition of whole numbers) using various representations.
description_plain: Your child can describe, continue, create, and change repeating and growing patterns using addition with whole numbers and different ways to show them.

subject: Math
description: draw conclusions about the data and make predictions based on the data.
description_plain: Your child can look at information collected and make guesses about what might happen next.

subject: Math
description: ask and answer questions about the data represented in pictographs and bar graphs (e.g., total number of data points represented, how many in each category, how many more or less are in one category than another). Pictograph keys will be limited to symbols representing 1, 2, 5, or 10 pieces of data and bar graphs will be limited to scales with increments in multiples of 1, 2, 5, or 10; and
description_plain: Your child can ask and answer questions about information shown in simple pictographs and bar graphs, like how many items are in each group and which group has more or less.

subject: Math
description: Analyze data represented in pictographs and bar graphs and communicate results:
description_plain: Your child will look at pictographs and bar graphs to understand the data and explain what it shows.

subject: Math
description: Organize and represent a data set using a bar graph with a title and labeled axes (limited to 25 or fewer data points for up to six categories, and limit increments of scale to multiples of 1 or 2).
description_plain: Your child can organize information and make a bar graph with a title and labels, using up to six groups and 25 or fewer pieces of data.

subject: Math
description: Organize and represent a data set using a pictograph where each symbol represents up to 2 data points. Determine and use a key to assist in the analysis of the data.
description_plain: Your child can organize information and make a pictograph using symbols that each stand for up to two items, and use a key to help understand the data.

subject: Math
description: Determine the data needed to answer a posed question and collect the data using various methods (e.g., voting; creating lists, tables, or charts; tallying).
description_plain: Your child can decide what information is needed to answer a question and collect that information by voting, making lists, tables, or tally marks.

subject: Math
description: Pose questions, given a predetermined context, that require the collection of data (limited to 25 or fewer data points for no more than six categories).
description_plain: Your child can ask questions that need information collected, using up to six groups and 25 or fewer pieces of data.

subject: Math
description: The student will apply the data cycle (pose questions; collect or acquire data; organize and represent data; and analyze data and communicate results) with a focus on pictographs and bar graphs.
description_plain: Your child understands how to ask questions, collect information, organize it in graphs, and explain what the data shows, focusing on pictographs and bar graphs.

subject: Math
description: Compare and contrast plane and solid figures (circles/spheres, squares/cubes, and rectangles/rectangular prisms) according to their characteristics (e.g., number and shapes of their faces, edges, vertices).
description_plain: Your child can compare flat shapes and solid shapes like circles and spheres or squares and cubes by looking at their parts like faces, edges, and corners.

subject: Math
description: Given a concrete or pictorial model, name and describe the solid figure (sphere, cube, and rectangular prism) by its characteristics (e.g., number of edges, number of vertices, shapes of faces).
description_plain: Your child can name solid shapes like spheres, cubes, and rectangular prisms and describe them by counting their edges, corners, and faces.

subject: Math
description: Compare and contrast models and nets (cutouts) of cubes and rectangular prisms (e.g., number and shapes of faces, edges, vertices).
description_plain: Your child can compare models and flat patterns of cubes and rectangular prisms by looking at their faces, edges, and corners.

subject: Math
description: Trace faces of solid figures (cubes and rectangular prisms) to create the set of plane figures related to the solid figure.
description_plain: Your child can trace the flat faces of solid shapes like cubes and rectangular prisms to see the shapes that make them up.

subject: Math
description: The student will describe, name, compare, and contrast plane and solid figures (circles/spheres, squares/cubes, and rectangles/rectangular prisms).
description_plain: Your child will describe, name, and compare flat shapes and solid shapes like circles and spheres or squares and cubes.

subject: Math
description: Describe the two resulting figures formed by a line of symmetry as being congruent (having the same shape and size).
description_plain: Your child understands that when a shape is folded along its line of symmetry, the two halves are the same size and shape.

subject: Math
description: Create figures with at least one line of symmetry using various concrete and pictorial representations.
description_plain: Your child can make shapes that have at least one line of symmetry using objects or drawings.

subject: Math
description: Explore a figure using a variety of tools (e.g., paper folding, geoboards, drawings) to show and justify a line of symmetry, if one exists.
description_plain: Your child can explore shapes using tools like paper folding or drawings to find and explain a line of symmetry if it has one.

subject: Math
description: The student will identify, describe, and create plane figures (including circles, triangles, squares, and rectangles) that have at least one line of symmetry and explain its relationship with congruency.
description_plain: Your child will find, describe, and make flat shapes with at least one line of symmetry and explain how the two halves match.

subject: Math
description: Match a written time (e.g., 1:35, 6:20, 9:05) to the time shown on an analog clock to the nearest five minutes.
description_plain: Your child can match a written time like 1:35 or 6:20 to the time shown on a clock with hands, rounding to the nearest five minutes.

subject: Math
description: Show, tell, and write time to the nearest five minutes, using analog and digital clocks.
description_plain: Your child can tell, show, and write time to the nearest five minutes using both clocks with hands and digital clocks.

subject: Math
description: Determine the unit of time (minutes, hours, days, or weeks) that is most appropriate when measuring a given activity or context and explain reasoning (e.g., Would you measure the time it takes to brush your teeth in minutes or hours?).
description_plain: Your child can decide whether to measure time in minutes, hours, days, or weeks for different activities and explain why.

subject: Math
description: Identify the number of minutes in an hour (60 minutes) and the number of hours in a day (24 hours).
description_plain: Your child knows that there are 60 minutes in an hour and 24 hours in a day.

subject: Math
description: The student will demonstrate an understanding of the concept of time to the nearest five minutes, using analog and digital clocks.
description_plain: Your child can tell time to the nearest five minutes using both analog and digital clocks.

subject: Math
description: the liquid volume of a container to the nearest cup, using a measuring cup.
description_plain: Your child can measure how much liquid is in a container to the nearest cup using a measuring cup.

subject: Math
description: the weight of an object to the nearest pound, using a scale; and
description_plain: Your child can weigh an object to the nearest pound using a scale.

subject: Math
description: the length of an object to the nearest inch, using a ruler;
description_plain: Your child can measure the length of an object to the nearest inch using a ruler.

subject: Math
description: Use U.S. Customary units to estimate, measure, and compare the two for reasonableness:
description_plain: Your child can use U.S. Customary units to guess, measure, and compare lengths, weights, and liquid amounts to see if the answers make sense.

subject: Math
description: identifying different types of measuring cups as instruments to measure liquid volume.
description_plain: Your child can recognize different kinds of measuring cups used to measure liquids.

subject: Math
description: identifying different types of scales as instruments to measure weight; and
description_plain: Your child can recognize different kinds of scales used to measure weight.

subject: Math
description: identifying a ruler as an instrument to measure length;
description_plain: Your child can recognize a ruler as a tool to measure length.

subject: Math
description: Explain the purpose of various measurement tools and how to use them appropriately by:
description_plain: Your child understands what different measurement tools are for and how to use them correctly.

subject: Math
description: The student will reason mathematically using standard units (U.S. Customary) with appropriate tools to estimate, measure, and compare objects by length, weight, and liquid volume to the nearest whole unit.
description_plain: Your child can use U.S. Customary units and tools to estimate, measure, and compare the length, weight, and liquid volume of objects to the nearest whole unit.

subject: Math
description: Represent and justify the relationship between values and expressions as equal or not equal using appropriate models and/or symbols (e.g., 9 + 24 = 10 + 23; 45 - 9 = 46 - 10; 15 +16 ≠ 31 +15).
description_plain: Your child can show and explain when two math expressions are equal or not equal using pictures or symbols.

subject: Math
description: Describe the not equal symbol (≠) as representing a relationship where expressions on either side of the not equal symbol represent different values and justify reasoning.
description_plain: Your child understands that the 'not equal' sign (≠) means two expressions have different values and can explain why.

subject: Math
description: Use inverse relationships to write all related facts connected to a given addition or subtraction fact model within 20 (e.g., given a model for 3 + 4 = 7, write 4 + 3 = 7, 7 – 4 = 3, and 7 – 3 = 4).
description_plain: Your child can write all related addition and subtraction facts for numbers up to 20 using what they know about inverse operations.

subject: Math
description: Determine the missing number in an equation (number sentence) through modeling and justification with addition and subtraction within 20 (e.g., 3 + = 5 or + 2 = 5; 5 – = 3 or 5 – 2 = ).
description_plain: Your child can find the missing number in addition or subtraction problems within 20 by using models and explaining their thinking.

subject: Math
description: Use patterns, models, and strategies to make generalizations about the algebraic properties for fluency (e.g., 4 + 3 is equal to 3 + 4; 0 + 8 = 8).
description_plain: Your child uses patterns and strategies to understand basic math properties, like knowing 4 + 3 is the same as 3 + 4.

subject: Math
description: Recall with automaticity addition and subtraction facts within 20.
description_plain: Your child can quickly recall addition and subtraction facts within 20 without needing to count.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate fluency with addition and subtraction within 20 by applying reasoning strategies (e.g., doubles, near doubles, make-a-ten, compensations, inverse relationships).
description_plain: Your child can solve addition and subtraction problems within 20 using smart strategies like doubles, making ten, or using inverse operations.

subject: Math
description: Represent, solve, and justify solutions to single-step and multistep contextual problems (e.g., join, separate, part-part-whole, comparison) involving addition or subtraction of whole numbers where addends or minuends do not exceed 100.
description_plain: Your child can solve and explain answers to one-step and multi-step word problems involving addition or subtraction with numbers up to 100.

subject: Math
description: Apply strategies (e.g., the use of concrete and pictorial models, place value, properties of addition, the relationship between addition and subtraction) to determine the sum or difference of two whole numbers where addends or minuends do not exceed 100.
description_plain: Your child uses tools like pictures, place value, and math properties to add or subtract two numbers up to 100.

subject: Math
description: Apply strategies (e.g., rounding to the nearest 10, compatible numbers, other number relationships) to estimate a solution for single-step addition or subtraction problems, including those in context, where addends and minuends do not exceed 100.
description_plain: Your child can estimate answers to addition or subtraction problems up to 100 by rounding or using number patterns.

subject: Math
description: The student will recall with automaticity addition and subtraction facts within 20 and estimate, represent, solve, and justify solutions to single-step and multistep problems, including those in context, using addition and subtraction with whole numbers where addends or minuends do not exceed 100.
description_plain: Your child can quickly recall addition and subtraction facts within 20 and solve one-step and multi-step problems using addition and subtraction with numbers up to 100.

subject: Math
description: Represent the value of a collection of coins and one-dollar bills (limited to $2.00 or less) using the cent (¢) and dollar ($) symbols and decimal point (.).
description_plain: Your child can show the value of coins and one-dollar bills up to $2.00 using the correct symbols like cents (¢), dollars ($), and decimal points.

subject: Math
description: Construct a set of coins and/or bills to total a given amount of money whose value is $2.00 or less.
description_plain: Your child can put together coins and bills to make a specific amount of money up to $2.00.

subject: Math
description: Count by ones, fives, tens, and twenty-fives to determine the value of a collection of mixed coins and one-dollar bills whose total value is $2.00 or less.
description_plain: Your child can count mixed coins and one-dollar bills up to $2.00 by counting ones, fives, tens, and twenty-fives.

subject: Math
description: Identify a quarter and its value and determine multiple ways to represent the value of a quarter using pennies, nickels, and/or dimes.
description_plain: Your child can recognize a quarter, know its value, and find different ways to make that value using pennies, nickels, and dimes.

subject: Math
description: The student will solve problems that involve counting and representing money amounts up to $2.00.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems that involve counting and showing money amounts up to $2.00.

subject: Math
description: Compare unit fractions for halves, fourths, eighths, thirds, and sixths using words (greater than, less than or equal to) and symbols (>, <, =), with region/area and length models.
description_plain: Your child can compare simple fractions like halves, fourths, eighths, thirds, and sixths using words and symbols, with help from pictures and length models.

subject: Math
description: set models (e.g., chips, counters, cubes).
description_plain: Your child can use objects like chips, counters, or cubes to show fractions.

subject: Math
description: length models (e.g., paper fraction strips, fraction bars, rods, number lines); and
description_plain: Your child can use paper strips, bars, rods, or number lines to show fractions.

subject: Math
description: region/area models (e.g., pie pieces, pattern blocks, geoboards);
description_plain: Your child can use shapes like pie pieces, blocks, or geoboards to show fractions.

subject: Math
description: Given a context, represent, name, and write fractional parts of a whole for halves, fourths, eighths, thirds, and sixths using:
description_plain: Your child can show, name, and write fractions like halves, fourths, eighths, thirds, and sixths using different models.

subject: Math
description: Using same-size fraction pieces, from a region/area model, count by unit fractions up to two wholes (e.g., zero one-fourths, one one-fourth, two one-fourths, three one-fourths, four onefourths, five one-fourths; or zero-fourths, one-fourth, two-fourths, three-fourths, four-fourths, five-fourths).
description_plain: Your child can count fractions using same-size pieces from zero up to two wholes, like counting one-fourths up to five-fourths.

subject: Math
description: Compose the whole for a given fractional part and its value (in context) for halves, fourths, eighths, thirds, and sixths (e.g., when given 1 4 , determine how many pieces would be needed to make 4 4 ).
description_plain: Your child can figure out how many fractional pieces make a whole, like knowing four one-fourths make one whole.

subject: Math
description: Describe the relationship between the number of fractional parts needed to make a whole and the size of the parts (i.e., as the whole is divided into more parts, each part becomes smaller).
description_plain: Your child understands that when a whole is divided into more parts, each part becomes smaller.

subject: Math
description: Model and describe fractions as representing equal-size parts of a whole.
description_plain: Your child can show and explain that fractions are equal parts of a whole.

subject: Math
description: The student will use mathematical reasoning and justification to solve contextual problems that involve partitioning models into equal-sized parts (halves, fourths, eighths, thirds, and sixths).
description_plain: Your child can solve problems by dividing shapes or objects into equal parts like halves, fourths, eighths, thirds, and sixths.

subject: Math
description: Order up to three whole numbers, each 999 or less, represented concretely, pictorially, or symbolically from least to greatest and greatest to least.
description_plain: Your child can put up to three whole numbers (each 999 or less) in order from smallest to largest and largest to smallest using objects, pictures, or numbers.

subject: Math
description: Compare two whole numbers, each 999 or less, represented concretely, pictorially, or symbolically, using words (greater than, less than, or equal to) and symbols (>, <, or =). Justify reasoning orally, in writing, or with a model.
description_plain: Your child can compare two whole numbers (each 999 or less) using words and symbols, and explain why one is greater, less, or equal to the other.

subject: Math
description: Plot and justify the position of a given number up to 100 on a number line with pre-marked benchmarks of 1s, 2s, 5s, 10s, or 25s.
description_plain: Your child can find and explain where a number up to 100 belongs on a number line marked with 1s, 2s, 5s, 10s, or 25s.

subject: Math
description: Compose and decompose whole numbers up to 200 by making connections between a variety of models (e.g., base 10 blocks, place value cards, presented orally, in expanded or standard form) and counting strategies (e.g., 156 can be 1 hundred, 5 tens, 6 ones; 1 hundred, 4 tens, 16 ones; 15 tens, 6 ones).
description_plain: Your child can break apart and put together numbers up to 200 using different tools like base 10 blocks, place value cards, or by saying the number in parts.

subject: Math
description: Investigate and explain the ten-to-one relationships among ones, tens, and hundreds, using models.
description_plain: Your child can use models to explore and explain how ten ones make one ten, and ten tens make one hundred.

subject: Math
description: Apply patterns within the base 10 system to determine and communicate, orally and in written form, the place (ones, tens, hundreds) and value of each digit in a three-digit whole number (e.g., in 352, the 5 represents 5 tens and its value is 50).
description_plain: Your child can find and explain the place and value of each digit in a three-digit number, like knowing the 5 in 352 means 50.

subject: Math
description: Read, write, and represent three-digit numbers in standard form, expanded form, and word form, using concrete or pictorial representations.
description_plain: Your child can read, write, and show three-digit numbers in normal form, broken down form, and word form using pictures or objects.

subject: Math
description: Write the three-digit whole number represented by a given model (e.g., concrete objects, pictures of base 10 blocks).
description_plain: Your child can write the three-digit number that matches a group of objects or pictures, like base 10 blocks.

subject: Math
description: The student will demonstrate an understanding of the ten-to-one relationships of the base 10 number system to represent, compare, and order whole numbers up to 999.
description_plain: Your child understands how numbers up to 999 are made using tens and ones, and can use this to compare and order numbers.

subject: Math
description: Determine whether a number (up to 50) is even or odd using concrete objects and justify reasoning (e.g., dividing collections of objects into two equal groups, pairing objects).
description_plain: Your child can tell if a number up to 50 is even or odd by grouping objects into two equal parts and explaining why.

subject: Math
description: Represent odd numbers (up to 50) with concrete objects, using two equal groups with one leftover or two equal addends plus 1.
description_plain: Your child can show odd numbers up to 50 using objects grouped in two equal parts with one left over or by adding two equal numbers plus one.

subject: Math
description: Represent even numbers (up to 50) with concrete objects, using two equal groups or two equal addends.
description_plain: Your child can show even numbers up to 50 by grouping objects into two equal parts or by adding two equal numbers.

subject: Math
description: Choose a reasonable estimate up to 1,000 when given a contextual problem (e.g., What would be the best estimate for the number of students in our school – 5, 50, or 500?).
description_plain: Your child can choose a good estimate up to 1,000 for real-life questions, like guessing how many students are in a school.

subject: Math
description: Describe and use patterns in skip counting backwards by 10s (from at least 200) to justify the next number in the counting sequence.
description_plain: Your child can skip count backwards by 10s from at least 200 and explain the pattern to find the next number.

subject: Math
description: Represent backward counting patterns when counting by groups of 10 from 200 or less using a variety of tools including objects, number lines, calculators, and hundreds charts.
description_plain: Your child can show counting backwards by 10s from 200 or less using tools like objects, number lines, or charts.

subject: Math
description: Represent forward counting patterns when counting by groups of 100 up to at least 1,000 starting at 0 using a variety of tools (e.g., objects, number lines, calculators, one thousand charts).
description_plain: Your child can count forward by 100s up to at least 1,000 starting at zero using objects, number lines, or charts.

subject: Math
description: Describe and use patterns in skip counting by multiples of 2 (to at least 50), and multiples of 5, 10, and 25 (to at least 200) to justify the next number in the counting sequence.
description_plain: Your child can skip count by 2s up to 50 and by 5s, 10s, and 25s up to 200, and explain the pattern to find the next number.

subject: Math
description: Represent forward counting patterns created when counting by groups of 5s, 10s, and 25s starting at various multiples up to at least 200 using a variety of tools (e.g., objects, number lines, hundreds charts).
description_plain: Your child can show counting forward by 5s, 10s, and 25s starting at different numbers up to 200 using objects, number lines, or charts.

subject: Math
description: Represent forward counting patterns when counting by groups of 2 up to at least 50, starting at various multiples of 2 and using a variety of tools (e.g., objects, number lines, hundreds charts).
description_plain: Your child can show counting forward by 2s up to 50 starting at different numbers using objects, number lines, or charts.

subject: Math
description: The student will utilize flexible counting strategies to determine and describe quantities up to 200.
description_plain: Your child can use different ways to count and describe amounts up to 200.

subject: Math
description: Investigate and explain the connection between two different representations of the same increasing or decreasing pattern.
description_plain: Your child can explore and explain how two different ways of showing the same growing or shrinking pattern are connected.

subject: Math
description: Create increasing and decreasing patterns using objects, pictures, numbers, and number lines.
description_plain: Your child can make patterns that go up or down using objects, pictures, numbers, or number lines.

subject: Math
description: Solve contextual problems that involve identifying, describing, and extending patterns.
description_plain: Your child can solve real problems by finding, describing, and continuing patterns.

subject: Math
description: Analyze an increasing or decreasing pattern and generalize the change to extend the pattern or identify missing terms using various representations.
description_plain: Your child can look at a pattern that goes up or down, figure out the rule, and use it to keep the pattern going or find missing parts.

subject: Math
description: Identify and describe increasing and decreasing patterns using various representations (e.g., objects, pictures, numbers, number lines).
description_plain: Your child can find and talk about patterns that go up or down using objects, pictures, numbers, or number lines.

subject: Math
description: The student will identify, describe, extend, and create increasing and decreasing patterns (limited to addition and subtraction of whole numbers), including those in context, using various representations.
description_plain: Your child will find, explain, continue, and make patterns that increase or decrease by adding or subtracting whole numbers, using different ways to show them.

subject: Math
description: solve one- and two-step addition and subtraction problems using data from pictographs and bar graphs.
description_plain: Your child can solve one- and two-step addition and subtraction problems using information from pictographs and bar graphs.

subject: Math
description: use characteristics of the data to draw conclusions about the data and make predictions based on the data (e.g., it is unlikely that a third grader would like hard boiled eggs); and
description_plain: Your child understands how to look at data to make conclusions and guesses about what might happen next.

subject: Math
description: make inferences about data represented in pictographs and bar graphs;
description_plain: Your child can make guesses and understand information from pictographs and bar graphs.

subject: Math
description: identify parts of the data that have special characteristics, including categories with the greatest, the least, or the same (e.g., most students prefer scrambled eggs);
description_plain: Your child can find parts of data that stand out, like which group is biggest, smallest, or equal.

subject: Math
description: describe the categories of data and the data as a whole (e.g., data were collected on preferred ways to cook or prepare eggs - scrambled, fried, hard boiled, and egg salad);
description_plain: Your child can describe the groups in data and explain what the data is about.

subject: Math
description: Analyze data represented in pictographs and bar graphs, and communicate results orally and in writing:
description_plain: Your child can study data shown in pictographs and bar graphs and talk or write about what the data means.

subject: Math
description: Organize and represent a data set using bar graphs with a title and labeled axes, with and without the use of technology tools. Determine and use an appropriate scale (increments limited to multiples of 1, 2, 5 or 10).
description_plain: Your child can organize data and make bar graphs with titles and labels, choosing the right scale using numbers like 1, 2, 5, or 10.

subject: Math
description: Organize and represent a data set using pictographs that include an appropriate title, labeled axes, and key. Each pictograph symbol should represent 1, 2, 5 or 10 data points.
description_plain: Your child can organize data and make pictographs with titles, labels, and keys, where each picture stands for 1, 2, 5, or 10 items.

subject: Math
description: Determine the data needed to answer a formulated question and collect or acquire existing data (limited to 30 or fewer data points for no more than eight categories) using various methods (e.g., polls, observations, tallies).
description_plain: Your child can decide what data is needed to answer a question and collect or find that data using ways like polls, watching, or tallying.

subject: Math
description: Formulate questions that require the collection or acquisition of data.
description_plain: Your child can come up with questions that need data to answer.

subject: Math
description: The student will apply the data cycle (formulate questions; collect or acquire data; organize and represent data; and analyze data and communicate results) with a focus on pictographs and bar graphs.
description_plain: Your child will practice asking questions, collecting data, organizing it, and studying it using pictographs and bar graphs.

subject: Math
description: Subdivide a three-sided or four-sided polygon into no more than three parts and name the resulting polygons.
description_plain: Your child can split a triangle or four-sided shape into up to three parts and name the new shapes.

subject: Math
description: Combine no more than three polygons, where each has three or four sides, and name the resulting polygon (triangles, quadrilaterals, pentagons, hexagons, octagons).
description_plain: Your child can put together up to three shapes with three or four sides and name the new shape they make.

subject: Math
description: Classify and compare polygons (triangles, quadrilaterals, pentagons, hexagons, octagons).
description_plain: Your child can sort and compare shapes like triangles, quadrilaterals, pentagons, hexagons, and octagons.

subject: Math
description: Identify and name examples of polygons (triangles, quadrilaterals, pentagons, hexagons, octagons) in the environment.
description_plain: Your child can find and name shapes like triangles, quadrilaterals, pentagons, hexagons, and octagons around them.

subject: Math
description: Identify and describe triangles, quadrilaterals, pentagons, hexagons, and octagons in various orientations, with and without contexts.
description_plain: Your child can recognize and describe triangles, quadrilaterals, pentagons, hexagons, and octagons in different positions and situations.

subject: Math
description: Classify figures as polygons or not polygons and justify reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can decide if a shape is a polygon or not and explain why.

subject: Math
description: Describe a polygon as a closed plane figure composed of at least three line segments that do not cross.
description_plain: Your child understands that a polygon is a flat, closed shape made of at least three straight lines that don’t cross.

subject: Math
description: The student will identify, describe, classify, compare, combine, and subdivide polygons.
description_plain: Your child can recognize, describe, group, compare, put together, and break apart shapes with straight sides called polygons.

subject: Math
description: the ending time and the amount of elapsed time in one-hour increments, determine the starting time.
description_plain: Your child can figure out what time something started when they know the ending time and how many whole hours passed.

subject: Math
description: the starting time and amount of elapsed time in one-hour increments, determine the ending time; or
description_plain: Your child can find out what time something ended when they know the starting time and how many whole hours passed.

subject: Math
description: the starting time and the ending time, determine the amount of time that has elapsed;
description_plain: Your child can tell how much time passed by knowing the start and end times.

subject: Math
description: Solve single-step contextual problems involving elapsed time in one-hour increments, within a 12-hour period (within a.m. or within p.m.) when given:
description_plain: Your child can solve simple real-life problems about how much time passed in whole hours within a 12-hour period, using start time, end time, or elapsed time.

subject: Math
description: Match a written time (e.g., 4:38, 7:09, 12:51) to the time shown on analog and digital clocks to the nearest minute.
description_plain: Your child can match a written time like 4:38 or 7:09 to what the clock shows on both analog and digital clocks, down to the nearest minute.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time to the nearest minute, using analog and digital clocks.
description_plain: Your child can tell and write the time to the nearest minute using both analog and digital clocks.

subject: Math
description: The student will demonstrate an understanding of the concept of time to the nearest minute and solve single-step contextual problems involving elapsed time in one-hour increments within a 12-hour period.
description_plain: Your child understands how to tell time to the nearest minute and can solve simple problems about elapsed time in whole hours within a 12-hour period.

subject: Math
description: given the lengths of all sides of a polygon (with no more than six sides), determine its perimeter and justify the measurement.
description_plain: Your child can find the total distance around a shape with up to six sides by adding all the side lengths and explain how they did it.

subject: Math
description: estimate and measure the distance around a polygon (with no more than six sides) to determine the perimeter and justify the measurement; and
description_plain: Your child can guess and then measure the distance around a shape with up to six sides to find its perimeter and explain their measurement.

subject: Math
description: describe and give examples of perimeter as a measurement in contextual situations;
description_plain: Your child can explain what perimeter means and give examples of measuring the distance around things.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems, including those in context, involving perimeter:
description_plain: Your child can solve problems about perimeter, including real-life situations.

subject: Math
description: estimate and determine the area of a given surface by counting the number of square units, describe the measurement (using the number and unit) and justify the measurement.
description_plain: Your child can guess and then find the area of a flat surface by counting square units, tell the number and type of units, and explain how they measured it.

subject: Math
description: describe and give examples of area as a measurement in contextual situations; and
description_plain: Your child can explain what area means and give examples of measuring the space inside shapes.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems, including those in context, involving area:
description_plain: Your child can solve problems about area, including real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: The student will use multiple representations to estimate and solve problems, including those in context, involving area and perimeter (in both U.S. Customary and metric units).
description_plain: Your child can use different ways to estimate and solve problems about area and perimeter, using both U.S. and metric units.

subject: Math
description: Compare estimates of length, weight/mass, or liquid volume with the actual measurements.
description_plain: Your child can compare their guesses about length, weight, or liquid amount to the actual measurements.

subject: Math
description: liquid volume to the nearest U.S. Customary unit (cup, pint, quart, gallon) and metric unit (liter).
description_plain: Your child can measure how much liquid is in something using cups, pints, quarts, gallons, or liters.

subject: Math
description: weight/mass of an object to the nearest U.S. Customary unit (pound) and metric unit (kilogram); and
description_plain: Your child can measure how heavy something is using pounds or kilograms.

subject: Math
description: length of an object to the nearest U.S. Customary unit (1 2 inch, inch, foot, yard) and metric unit (centimeter, meter);
description_plain: Your child can measure how long something is using inches, feet, yards, centimeters, or meters.

subject: Math
description: Justify whether an estimate or an exact measurement is needed for a contextual situation and choose an appropriate unit.
description_plain: Your child can decide when to use an estimate or an exact measurement in real-life situations and pick the right unit to measure.

subject: Math
description: The student will reason mathematically using standard units (U.S. Customary and metric) with appropriate tools to estimate and measure objects by length, weight/mass, and liquid volume to the nearest half or whole unit.
description_plain: Your child will use tools and standard units like inches, pounds, or liters to estimate and measure length, weight, and liquid volume to the nearest half or whole unit.

subject: Math
description: Create an equation to represent the mathematical relationship between equivalent expressions using multiplication and/or division facts through 10 × 10 (e.g., 4 × 3 = 14 - 2, 35 ÷ 5 = 1 × 7).
description_plain: Your child can write equations that show how multiplication and division facts up to 10 × 10 are equal, like 4 × 3 = 14 - 2.

subject: Math
description: Recall with automaticity the multiplication facts through 10 × 10 and the corresponding division facts.
description_plain: Your child will quickly recall multiplication and division facts up to 10 × 10 without needing to think hard.

subject: Math
description: Represent, solve, and justify solutions to single-step contextual problems that involve multiplication and division of whole numbers through 10 × 10.
description_plain: Your child can solve and explain answers to one-step word problems using multiplication and division facts up to 10 × 10.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate fluency with multiplication facts through 10 × 10 by applying reasoning strategies (e.g., doubling, add-a-group, subtract-a-group, near squares, and inverse relationships).
description_plain: Your child will use smart ways like doubling or using related facts to quickly remember multiplication facts up to 10 × 10.

subject: Math
description: Apply strategies (e.g., place value, the properties of multiplication and/or addition) when multiplying and dividing whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child can use helpful tricks like place value and properties of multiplication or addition when multiplying or dividing whole numbers.

subject: Math
description: Use inverse relationships to write the related facts connected to a given model for multiplication and division of whole numbers through 10 × 10.
description_plain: Your child can write related multiplication and division facts by using the idea that they undo each other, for numbers up to 10 × 10.

subject: Math
description: Represent multiplication and division of whole numbers through 10 × 10, including in a contextual situation, using a variety of approaches and models (e.g., repeated addition/subtraction, equal-sized groups/sharing, arrays, equal jumps on a number line, using multiples to skip count).
description_plain: Your child can show multiplication and division facts up to 10 × 10 in different ways, like groups, arrays, or jumps on a number line, including in real-life problems.

subject: Math
description: The student will recall with automaticity multiplication and division facts through 10 × 10; and represent, solve, and justify solutions to single-step contextual problems using multiplication and division with whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child will quickly remember multiplication and division facts up to 10 × 10 and solve one-step word problems using these facts.

subject: Math
description: Represent, solve, and justify solutions to single-step and multistep contextual problems involving addition and subtraction with whole numbers where addends and minuends do not exceed 1,000.
description_plain: Your child can solve and explain answers to one-step and multi-step word problems using addition and subtraction with numbers up to 1,000.

subject: Math
description: Identify and use the appropriate symbol to distinguish between expressions that are equal and expressions that are not equal (e.g., 256 - 13 = 220 + 23; 457 + 100 ≠ 557 + 100).
description_plain: Your child can tell when two math expressions are equal or not equal and use the right symbol (= or ≠) to show it.

subject: Math
description: Apply strategies (e.g., place value, properties of addition, other number relationships) and algorithms, including the standard algorithm, to determine the sum or difference of two whole numbers where addends and minuends do not exceed 1,000.
description_plain: Your child can use strategies and steps, including the usual method, to add or subtract two numbers up to 1,000.

subject: Math

subject: Math
description: Determine and justify whether an estimate or an exact answer is appropriate when solving single-step and multistep contextual problems involving addition and subtraction, where addends and minuends do not exceed 1,000.

subject: Math
description: The student will estimate, represent, solve, and justify solutions to single-step and multistep problems, including those in context, using addition and subtraction with whole numbers where addends and minuends do not exceed 1,000.
description_plain: Your child will estimate, solve, and explain answers to one-step and multi-step addition and subtraction problems with numbers up to 1,000, including real-life problems.

subject: Math
description: Solve contextual problems to make change from $5.00 or less by using counting on or counting back strategies with concrete or pictorial models.

description_plain: Your child can solve money problems up to $5.00, including counting, comparing amounts, and making change.

subject: Math
description: Represent equivalent fractions with denominators of 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, or 10, using region/area models and length models.

subject: Math
description: Compare two fractions (proper or improper) and/or mixed numbers with like denominators of 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, and 10 (e.g., 3 6 < 4 6 ) using words (greater than, less than, equal to) and/or symbols (>, <, =), using area/region models, length models, and without models.

subject: Math
description: Compare two fractions (proper or improper) and/or mixed numbers with like numerators of 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, and 10 (e.g., 2 3 > 2 8 ) using words (greater than, less than, equal to) and/or symbols (>, <, =), using area/region models, length models, and without models.

description_plain: Your child can look at number patterns that go up or down, figure out the rule, continue the pattern, or find missing numbers.

subject: Math

description_plain: Your child understands how to find, describe, continue, and create number patterns that increase or decrease using objects, pictures, numbers, and tables.

subject: Math
description: Create a model or contextual problem to represent a given probability.

description_plain: Your child understands probability as how likely something is to happen, using words like impossible, unlikely, likely, or certain.

subject: Math
description: The student will model and determine the probability of an outcome of a simple event.
description_plain: Your child can use models to find and understand the chance of a simple event happening.

subject: Math
description: solve single-step and multistep addition and subtraction problems using data from line graphs.

subject: Math
