description_plain: Your child can decide when to use an estimate or an exact answer for multiplication and division problems and can improve estimates by explaining if the answer is closer to or a bit more than a number.

subject: Math
description: The student will estimate, represent, solve, and justify solutions to single-step and multistep problems, including those in context, using multiplication with whole numbers, and single-step problems, including those in context, using division with whole numbers; and recall with automaticity the multiplication facts through 12 × 12 and the corresponding division facts.
description_plain: Your child can estimate, solve, and explain answers to one-step and multi-step multiplication and division problems with whole numbers and quickly recall multiplication and division facts up to 12 × 12.

subject: Math
description: Estimate, represent, solve, and justify solutions to single-step and multistep contextual problems involving addition and subtraction with whole numbers where addends and minuends do not exceed 1,000,000.
description_plain: Your child can estimate, solve, and explain answers to one-step and multi-step addition and subtraction problems with numbers up to 1,000,000.

subject: Math
description: Apply strategies (e.g., place value, properties of addition, other number relationships) and algorithms, including the standard algorithm, to determine the sum or difference of two whole numbers, where addends and minuends do not exceed 10,000.*
description_plain: Your child can use strategies and standard methods to find the sum or difference of two whole numbers up to 10,000.

subject: Math
description: Apply strategies (e.g., rounding to the nearest 100 or 1,000, using compatible numbers, other number relationships) to estimate a solution for single-step or multistep addition or subtraction problems with whole numbers, where addends or minuends do not exceed 10,000.*
description_plain: Your child can use rounding and other strategies to estimate answers for addition or subtraction problems with numbers up to 10,000.

subject: Math
description: Determine and justify whether an estimate or an exact answer is appropriate when solving contextual problems involving addition and subtraction with whole numbers. Refine estimates by adjusting the final amount, using terms such as closer to, between, and a little more than.
description_plain: Your child can decide when to estimate or find the exact answer for addition and subtraction problems and can improve estimates by explaining if the answer is closer to or a bit more than a number.

subject: Math
description: The student will estimate, represent, solve, and justify solutions to single-step and multistep problems, including those in context, using addition and subtraction with whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child can estimate, solve, and explain answers to one-step and multi-step addition and subtraction problems with whole numbers.

subject: Math
description: Write the decimal and fraction equivalent for a given model (e.g., 1 4 = 0.25 or 0.25 = 1 4 ; 1.25 = 5 4 or 11 4 ; 1.02 = 102 100 or 1 2 100 ).*
description_plain: Your child can write decimals and fractions that mean the same thing for a given model, like showing 0.25 equals 1/4.

subject: Math
description: Identify and model equivalent relationships between fractions (proper or improper) and/or mixed numbers and decimals, using halves, fourths, fifths, tenths, and hundredths.*
description_plain: Your child can recognize and show how fractions and decimals like halves, fourths, fifths, tenths, and hundredths are equal using models.

subject: Math
description: Represent fractions (proper or improper) and/or mixed numbers as decimals through hundredths, using multiple representations, limited to halves, fourths, fifths, tenths, and hundredths.*
description_plain: Your child can show fractions and mixed numbers as decimals up to hundredths using different ways like pictures and numbers.

subject: Math
description: The student will reason about the relationship between fractions and decimals (limited to halves, fourths, fifths, tenths, and hundredths) to identify and represent equivalencies.
description_plain: Your child understands how fractions and decimals (like halves, fourths, fifths, tenths, and hundredths) are related and can show when they are equal.

subject: Math
description: Compare using symbols (<, >, =) and/or words (greater than, less than, equal to) and order (least to greatest and greatest to least), a set of no more than four decimals expressed through thousandths, using multiple strategies (e.g., benchmarks, place value, number lines). Justify comparisons with a model, orally, and in writing.
description_plain: Your child can compare up to four decimals through thousandths using symbols or words and explain their thinking with models and words.

subject: Math
description: Identify and communicate, both orally and in written form, the place and value of each digit in a decimal through thousandths (e.g., given 0.385, the 8 is in the hundredths place and has a value of 0.08).
description_plain: Your child can say and write the place and value of each digit in a decimal number up to thousandths, like knowing the 8 in 0.385 means 0.08.

subject: Math
description: Read and write decimals expressed through thousandths, using concrete, pictorial, and numerical representations.
description_plain: Your child can read and write decimals up to thousandths using objects, pictures, and numbers.

subject: Math
description: Represent and identify decimals expressed through thousandths, using concrete, pictorial, and numerical representations.
description_plain: Your child can show and recognize decimals up to thousandths using objects, pictures, and numbers.

subject: Math
description: Investigate and describe the ten-to-one place value relationship for decimals through thousandths, using concrete models (e.g., place value mats/charts, decimal squares, base 10 blocks).
description_plain: Your child will explore and explain how each place in a decimal is ten times smaller than the one before, using things like blocks and charts.

subject: Math
description: The student will use mathematical reasoning and justification to represent, compare, and order decimals through thousandths, with and without models.
description_plain: Your child can explain, compare, and put decimals up to thousandths in order, using or without using models.

subject: Math
description: Represent the division of two whole numbers as a fraction given a contextual situation and a model (e.g., 3 5 means the same as 3 divided by 5 or 3 5 represents the amount of muffin each of five children will receive when sharing three muffins equally).
description_plain: Your child can show division of whole numbers as fractions using real-life examples and models, like sharing muffins equally.

subject: Math
description: Compose and decompose fractions (proper and improper) and/or mixed numbers with denominators of 12 or less, in multiple ways, with and without models.*
description_plain: Your child can break apart and put together fractions and mixed numbers with denominators up to 12 in different ways, with or without models.

subject: Math
description: Represent equivalent fractions with denominators of 12 or less, with and without models.*
description_plain: Your child can show fractions that are equal to each other with denominators up to 12, using or without using models.

subject: Math
description: Compare two fractions (proper or improper) and/or mixed numbers using fractions with denominators of 12 or less, using the symbols >, <, and = (e.g., 2 3 > 1 7 ). Justify comparisons orally, in writing, or with a model.*
description_plain: Your child can compare two fractions or mixed numbers with denominators up to 12 using >, <, or = and explain why.

subject: Math
description: Use benchmarks (e.g., 0, 1 2 , or 1) to compare and order no more than four fractions (proper or improper), and/or mixed numbers, with like and unlike denominators of 12 or less. Justify comparisons orally, in writing, or with a model.*
description_plain: Your child can use easy-to-understand fractions like 0, 1/2, or 1 to compare and order up to four fractions or mixed numbers with denominators up to 12 and explain their thinking.

subject: Math
description: Compare and order no more than four fractions (proper or improper), and/or mixed numbers, with like numerators and unlike denominators by comparing the size of the parts using fractions with denominators of 12 or less (e.g., 3 8 < 3 5 ). Justify comparisons orally, in writing, or with a model.*
description_plain: Your child can compare and order up to four fractions or mixed numbers that have the same top number but different bottom numbers (up to 12) and explain why.

subject: Math
description: Compare and order no more than four fractions (proper or improper), and/or mixed numbers, with like denominators by comparing the number of parts (numerators) using fractions with denominators of 12 or less (e.g., 1 5 < 3 5 ). Justify comparisons orally, in writing, or with a model.*
description_plain: Your child can compare and order up to four fractions or mixed numbers that have the same bottom number (up to 12) by looking at the top numbers and explain their thinking.

subject: Math
description: The student will use mathematical reasoning and justification to represent, compare, and order fractions (proper, improper, and mixed numbers with denominators 12 or less), with and without models.
description_plain: Your child can explain, compare, and put in order fractions and mixed numbers with denominators up to 12, using or without models.

subject: Math
description: Order up to four whole numbers up to seven digits each, from least to greatest or greatest to least.
description_plain: Your child can put up to four whole numbers, each up to seven digits long, in order from smallest to largest or largest to smallest.

subject: Math
description: Compare two whole numbers up to seven digits each, using words (greater than, less than, equal to, not equal to) and/or using symbols (>, <, =, ≠).
description_plain: Your child can compare two whole numbers up to seven digits using words like greater than or less than, and symbols like > or <.

subject: Math
description: The student will demonstrate an understanding of the base 10 system to compare and order whole numbers up to seven digits.
description_plain: Your child understands how the base 10 system works to compare and order whole numbers up to seven digits.

subject: Math
description: Apply patterns within the base 10 system to determine and communicate, orally and in written form, the place and value of each digit in a nine-digit whole number (e.g., in 568,165,724, the 8 represents 8 millions and its value is 8,000,000).
description_plain: Your child can explain the place and value of each digit in a nine-digit number, like knowing that the 8 in 568,165,724 means 8 million.

subject: Math
description: Write nine-digit whole numbers in standard form when the numbers are presented orally or in written form.
description_plain: Your child can write nine-digit numbers in normal number form when they hear or see them written out.

subject: Math
description: Read nine-digit whole numbers, presented in standard form, and represent the same number in written form.
description_plain: Your child can read nine-digit numbers and write them out in words.

subject: Math
description: The student will use place value understanding to read, write, and identify the place and value of each digit in a nine-digit whole number.
description_plain: Your child understands place value to read, write, and know the place and value of each digit in nine-digit numbers.

subject: Math
description: Create and write a word problem to match a given equation with a single variable and one operation.
description_plain: Your child can write a word problem that matches a math equation with one variable and one operation.

subject: Math
description: Use an expression with a variable to represent a given verbal expression involving one operation (e.g., “5 more than a number” can be represented by y + 5).
description_plain: Your child can write a math expression with a variable to show a simple phrase, like '5 more than a number' as y + 5.

subject: Math
description: Write an equation (with a single variable that represents an unknown quantity and one operation) from a contextual situation, using addition, subtraction, multiplication, or division.
description_plain: Your child can write a simple math equation with one unknown number and one operation based on a real-life situation.

subject: Math
description: Describe the concept of a variable (presented as a box, letter, or other symbol) as a representation of an unknown quantity.
description_plain: Your child understands that a variable is a symbol, like a letter or box, that stands for a number we don’t know yet.

subject: Math
description: The student will investigate and use variables in contextual problems.
description_plain: Your child uses variables to solve problems that come from real-life situations.

subject: Math
description: Solve contextual problems that involve identifying, describing, and extending increasing and decreasing patterns using single-operation input and output rules (limited to addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division of whole numbers; addition and subtraction of fractions with like denominators of 12 or less; and addition and subtraction of decimals expressed in tenths or hundredths).
description_plain: Your child solves problems by finding and continuing patterns that go up or down, using addition, subtraction, multiplication, or division with whole numbers, fractions, or decimals.

subject: Math
description: Analyze an increasing or decreasing single-operation numerical pattern found in lists, input/output tables, and function machines, and generalize the change to identify the rule, extend the pattern, or identify missing terms. (Patterns will be limited to addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division of whole numbers; addition and subtraction of fractions with like denominators of 12 or less; and addition and subtraction of decimals expressed in tenths or hundredths).
description_plain: Your child looks at number patterns in lists or tables, finds the rule that changes the numbers, and uses it to continue the pattern or find missing numbers.

subject: Math
description: Identify, describe, extend, and create increasing and decreasing patterns using various representations (e.g., objects, pictures, numbers, number lines, input/output tables, function machines).
description_plain: Your child can find, describe, continue, and make patterns that increase or decrease using objects, pictures, numbers, or tables.

subject: Math
description: The student will identify, describe, extend, and create increasing and decreasing patterns with whole numbers, fractions, and decimals, including those in context, using various representations.
description_plain: Your child works with patterns that go up or down using whole numbers, fractions, and decimals, and shows these patterns in different ways.

subject: Math
description: Determine the number of possible outcomes by using the Fundamental (Basic) Counting Principle.
description_plain: Your child can figure out how many different outcomes are possible by using a basic counting method.

subject: Math
description: Determine the probability of an outcome by constructing a sample space (with a total of 24 or fewer equally likely possible outcomes), using a tree diagram, list, or chart to represent and determine all possible outcomes.
description_plain: Your child finds the chance of something happening by listing all possible outcomes (up to 24) using a tree diagram, list, or chart.

subject: Math
description: The student will determine the probability of an outcome by constructing a model of a sample space and using the Fundamental (Basic) Counting Principle.
description_plain: Your child finds the chance of an event by making a model of all possible outcomes and using a basic counting rule.

subject: Math
description: Describe and determine the range of a set of data values representing data from a given context as a measure of spread.
description_plain: Your child can find and explain the range, which shows how spread out a set of numbers is, from real-life data.

subject: Math
description: Describe and determine the mode of a set of data values representing data from a given context as a measure of center.
description_plain: Your child can find and explain the mode, which is the number that appears most often, from real-life data.

subject: Math
description: Describe and determine the median of a set of data values representing data from a given context as a measure of center.
description_plain: Your child can find and explain the median, which is the middle number, from real-life data.

subject: Math
description: Describe and determine the mean of a set of data values representing data from a given context as a measure of center.
description_plain: Your child can find and explain the mean, which is the average, from real-life data.

subject: Math
description: The student will solve contextual problems using measures of center and the range.
description_plain: Your child solves real-life problems by using the average, middle, most common numbers, and the range from data.

subject: Math
description: solve single-step and multistep addition and subtraction problems using data from line plots (dot plots) and stem-and-leaf plots.
description_plain: Your child solves one-step and multi-step addition and subtraction problems using information from line plots and stem-and-leaf plots.

subject: Math
description: draw conclusions about the data and make predictions based on the data to answer questions; and
description_plain: Your child looks at data and uses it to answer questions and make predictions.

subject: Math
description: identify parts of the data that have special characteristics and explain the meaning of the greatest, the least, or the same (e.g., the stem-and-leaf plot shows that the same number of students scored in the 90s as scored in the 70s);
description_plain: Your child finds special parts of data and explains what the highest, lowest, or equal numbers mean.

subject: Math
description: make inferences about data represented in line plots (dot plots) and stem-and-leaf plots (e.g., based on a line plot (dot plot) of the number of books students in a bus line have in their backpack, every student will have from two to four books in their backpack);
description_plain: Your child makes guesses about data shown in line plots and stem-and-leaf plots, like how many books students have.

subject: Math
description: describe the characteristics of the data represented in a line plot (dot plot) and stem-andleaf plot as a whole (e.g., the shape and spread of the data);
description_plain: Your child describes the overall look and spread of data shown in line plots and stem-and-leaf plots.

subject: Math
description: Analyze data represented in line plots (dot plots) and stem-and-leaf plots and communicate results orally and in writing:
description_plain: Your child can look at data shown in line plots and stem-and-leaf plots and explain what the data means out loud and in writing.

subject: Math
description: Organize and represent numerical data using a stem-and-leaf plot with a title and key, where the stems are listed in ascending order and the leaves are in ascending order, with or without commas between the leaves.
description_plain: Your child can organize numbers into a stem-and-leaf plot with a clear title and key, putting the stems and leaves in order from smallest to largest.

subject: Math
description: Organize and represent a data set using a line plot (dot plot) with a title, labeled axes, and a key, with and without the use of technology tools. Lines plots (dot plots) may contain whole numbers, fractions, or decimals.
description_plain: Your child can create a line plot (dot plot) with a title, labeled axes, and a key, using whole numbers, fractions, or decimals, and can do this with or without technology.

subject: Math
description: Determine the data needed to answer a formulated question and collect or acquire existing data (limited to 30 or fewer data points) using various methods (e.g., polls, observations, measurements, experiments).
description_plain: Your child can decide what data is needed to answer a question and collect or find up to 30 data points using methods like polls, observations, or experiments.

subject: Math
description: Formulate questions that require the collection or acquisition of data.
description_plain: Your child can come up with questions that need data to be collected or found to answer them.

subject: Math
description: The student will apply the data cycle (formulate questions; collect or acquire data; organize and represent data; and analyze data and communicate results) with a focus on line plots (dot plots) and stem-and-leaf plots.
description_plain: Your child understands how to ask questions, gather data, organize it, and explain what the data shows, especially using line plots and stem-and-leaf plots.

subject: Math
description: Solve addition and subtraction contextual problems to determine unknown angle measures on a diagram.
description_plain: Your child can solve story problems by adding or subtracting to find missing angle measurements on a picture.

subject: Math
description: Use models to prove that the sum of the interior angles of a triangle is 180 degrees and use the relationship to determine an unknown angle measure in a triangle.
description_plain: Your child can use shapes to show that the inside angles of a triangle add up to 180 degrees and use this to find missing angles.

subject: Math
description: Measure right, acute, obtuse, and straight angles, using appropriate tools, and identify measures in degrees.
description_plain: Your child can measure different types of angles—right, acute, obtuse, and straight—using tools and tell their size in degrees.

subject: Math
description: Identify the appropriate tools (e.g., protractor, straightedge, angle ruler, available technology) to measure and draw angles.
description_plain: Your child can choose the right tools like a protractor or ruler to measure and draw angles.

subject: Math
description: Compare and contrast the properties of triangles.
description_plain: Your child can compare different triangles and explain how they are alike and different.

subject: Math
description: Identify congruent sides and right angles using geometric markings to denote properties of triangles.
description_plain: Your child can recognize equal sides and right angles in triangles by using special marks.

subject: Math
description: Classify triangles as right, acute, or obtuse and equilateral, scalene, or isosceles and justify reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can sort triangles by their angle types (right, acute, obtuse) and side types (equilateral, scalene, isosceles) and explain why.

subject: Math
description: Classify angles as right, acute, obtuse, or straight and justify reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can identify angles as right, acute, obtuse, or straight and explain their choices.

subject: Math
description: The student will classify and measure angles and triangles, and solve problems, including those in context.
description_plain: Your child can sort and measure angles and triangles and solve related problems, including real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Solve contextual problems that involve perimeter, area, and volume in standard units of measure.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-world problems involving perimeter, area, and volume using standard units.

subject: Math
description: Identify whether the application of the concept of perimeter, area, or volume is appropriate for a given situation.
description_plain: Your child can decide when to use perimeter, area, or volume to solve a problem.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems, including those in context, to estimate and determine the volume of a rectangular prism using concrete objects, diagrams, and formulas when the length, width, and height are given in whole number units. Record the solution with the appropriate unit of measure (e.g., 12 cubic inches).
description_plain: Your child can find the volume of a rectangular box by estimating or calculating using objects, pictures, or formulas, and write the answer with the right units.

subject: Math
description: Investigate and develop a formula for determining the volume of rectangular prisms using concrete objects.
description_plain: Your child can explore and create a formula to find the volume of rectangular boxes using real objects.

subject: Math
description: Describe volume as a measure of capacity and give examples of volume as a measurement in contextual situations.
description_plain: Your child understands that volume measures how much space something takes up and can give examples from real life.

subject: Math
description: Estimate and determine the area of a right triangle, with diagrams, when the base and the height are given in whole number units, in metric or U.S. Customary units, and record the solution with the appropriate unit of measure (e.g., 16 square inches).
description_plain: Your child can find and estimate the area of a right triangle when given the base and height as whole numbers, and write the answer with the correct unit like square inches.

subject: Math
description: Investigate and develop a formula for determining the area of a right triangle.
description_plain: Your child will explore and create a rule for how to find the area of a right triangle.

subject: Math
description: The student will use multiple representations to solve problems, including those in context, involving perimeter, area, and volume.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems about perimeter, area, and volume using pictures, numbers, and words.

subject: Math
description: liquid volume (milliliters and liters).
description_plain: Your child understands how to measure liquid volume using milliliters and liters.

subject: Math
description: mass (grams and kilograms); and
description_plain: Your child understands how to measure mass using grams and kilograms.

subject: Math
description: length (millimeters, centimeters, meters, and kilometers);
description_plain: Your child understands how to measure length using millimeters, centimeters, meters, and kilometers.

subject: Math
description: Given the equivalent metric measure of one unit, in a contextual problem, determine the equivalent measurement within the metric system:
description_plain: Your child can find equivalent measurements within the metric system when given one unit in a real-life problem.

subject: Math
description: liquid volume (milliliters and liters).
description_plain: Your child can estimate and measure liquid volume using milliliters and liters to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: mass (grams and kilograms); and
description_plain: Your child can estimate and measure mass using grams and kilograms to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: length (millimeters, centimeters, and meters);
description_plain: Your child can estimate and measure length using millimeters, centimeters, and meters to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Estimate and measure to solve contextual problems that involve metric units:
description_plain: Your child can estimate and measure metric units to solve real-world problems.

subject: Math
description: liquid volume (milliliters and liters).
description_plain: Your child can choose the best unit to measure liquid volume, like milliliters or liters, in a problem.

subject: Math
description: mass (grams and kilograms); and
description_plain: Your child can choose the best unit to measure mass, like grams or kilograms, in a problem.

subject: Math
description: length (millimeters, centimeters, meters, and kilometers);
description_plain: Your child can choose the best unit to measure length, like millimeters, centimeters, meters, or kilometers, in a problem.

subject: Math
description: Determine the most appropriate unit of measure to use in a contextual problem that involves metric units:
description_plain: Your child can decide which metric unit is best to use when solving a real-life measurement problem.

subject: Math
description: The student will reason mathematically to solve problems, including those in context, that involve length, mass, and liquid volume using metric units.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems about length, mass, and liquid volume using metric units.

subject: Math
description: Given a whole number numerical expression involving more than one operation, describe which operation is completed first, which is second, and which is third.*
description_plain: Your child can explain the order to do math operations in a problem with more than one step.

subject: Math
description: expressions should not include braces, brackets, or fraction bars.
description_plain: Your child will work with math expressions that do not use braces, brackets, or fraction bars.

subject: Math
description: whole numbers will be limited to two digits or less; and
description_plain: Your child will work with whole numbers that have two digits or less.

subject: Math
description: simplification will be limited to five whole numbers and four operations in any combination of addition, subtraction, multiplication, or division;
description_plain: Your child will simplify math problems using up to five whole numbers and four operations like adding, subtracting, multiplying, or dividing.

subject: Math
description: expressions may contain no more than one set of parentheses;
description_plain: Your child can work with math expressions that have only one set of parentheses.

subject: Math
description: Use order of operations to simplify numerical expressions with whole numbers, limited to addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division in which:*
description_plain: Your child can use the order of operations to simplify math problems with whole numbers using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division.

subject: Math
description: The student will simplify numerical expressions with whole numbers using the order of operations.
description_plain: Your child will simplify math expressions with whole numbers by following the correct order of operations.

subject: Math
description: Solve single-step contextual problems involving division with decimals by applying strategies (e.g., estimation, modeling) and algorithms, including the standard algorithm.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life problems that need one step to divide decimal numbers using methods like estimation, drawing models, or standard division.

subject: Math
description: Solve single-step and multistep contextual problems involving addition, subtraction, and multiplication of decimals by applying strategies (e.g., estimation, modeling) and algorithms, including the standard algorithm.
description_plain: Your child can solve one-step and multi-step real-life problems with adding, subtracting, and multiplying decimals using strategies like estimation, models, or standard methods.

subject: Math
description: no more than one additional zero will need to be annexed.
description_plain: Your child understands that sometimes only one extra zero needs to be added when dividing decimals.

subject: Math
description: divisors are limited to a single digit whole number or a decimal expressed as tenths; and
description_plain: Your child works with divisors that are either single-digit whole numbers or decimals with one digit after the decimal point.

subject: Math
description: quotients may include whole numbers, tenths, hundredths, or thousandths;
description_plain: Your child can find answers that include whole numbers or decimals up to three places (tenths, hundredths, or thousandths) when dividing.

subject: Math
description: quotients do not exceed four digits with or without a decimal point;
description_plain: Your child finds division answers that have up to four digits, with or without a decimal point.

subject: Math
description: Estimate and determine the quotient of two numbers using strategies and algorithms, including the standard algorithm, in which:*
description_plain: Your child can estimate and calculate division answers using different methods, including the standard way, with the rules about digits and decimals described.

subject: Math
description: a two-digit factor and a two-digit factor (e.g., 0.85 × 3.7, 14 × 1.6, 9.2 × 3.5).* (Products will not exceed the thousandths place, and leading zeroes will not be considered when counting digits.)
description_plain: Your child can multiply two numbers that each have two digits, like 0.85 × 3.7 or 14 × 1.6, and get answers up to three decimal places.

subject: Math
description: a three-digit factor and a one-digit factor (e.g., 0.156 × 4, 3.28 × 7, 8.09 × 0.2);* and
description_plain: Your child can multiply a three-digit number by a one-digit number, like 0.156 × 4 or 3.28 × 7.

subject: Math
description: a two-digit factor and a one-digit factor (e.g., 2.3 × 4; 0.08 × 0.9; .16 × 5);*
description_plain: Your child can multiply a two-digit number by a one-digit number, like 2.3 × 4 or 0.08 × 0.9.

subject: Math
description: Estimate and determine the product of two numbers using strategies and algorithms, including the standard algorithm, when given:
description_plain: Your child can estimate and find the product of two numbers using different methods, including the standard multiplication method, with the examples given.

subject: Math
description: Apply estimation strategies (e.g., rounding to the nearest whole number, tenth or hundredth; compatible numbers, place value) to determine a reasonable solution for single-step and multistep contextual problems involving addition, subtraction, and multiplication of decimals, and single-step contextual problems involving division of decimals.
description_plain: Your child can use estimation methods like rounding or place value to find reasonable answers for problems with adding, subtracting, multiplying, and dividing decimals.

subject: Math
description: The student will estimate, represent, solve, and justify solutions to single-step and multistep problems, including those in context, using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division with decimal numbers.
description_plain: Your child will estimate, solve, and explain answers to one-step and multi-step problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division with decimals.

subject: Math
description: Solve single-step contextual problems involving multiplication of a whole number, limited to 12 or less, and a proper fraction (e.g., 9 × 2 3 , 8 × 3 4 ), with models. The denominator will be a factor of the whole number and answers should be expressed in simplest form.*
description_plain: Your child can solve one-step problems multiplying a whole number (up to 12) by a proper fraction using models, and write the answer in simplest form.

subject: Math
description: Estimate and solve single-step and multistep contextual problems involving addition and subtraction with fractions (proper or improper) and/or mixed numbers having like and unlike denominators, with and without models. Denominators should be limited to 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, and 12. Answers should be expressed in simplest form.
description_plain: Your child can estimate and solve one-step and multi-step problems adding and subtracting fractions and mixed numbers with different denominators, using models when needed, and simplify answers.

subject: Math
description: Estimate and determine the sum or difference of two fractions (proper or improper) and/or mixed numbers, having like and unlike denominators limited to 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, and 12 (e.g., 5 8 + 1 4 , 4 5 − 2 3 , 33 4 + 2 5 12 ), and simplify the resulting fraction.*
description_plain: Your child can estimate and find the sum or difference of two fractions or mixed numbers with like or unlike denominators, and simplify the answer.

subject: Math
description: Determine the least common multiple of two numbers to find the least common denominator for two fractions.
description_plain: Your child can find the smallest number that two numbers both divide into, to help add or subtract fractions with different denominators.

subject: Math
description: The student will estimate, represent, solve, and justify solutions to single-step and multistep problems, including those in context, using addition and subtraction of fractions with like and unlike denominators (with and without models), and solve single-step contextual problems involving multiplication of a whole number and a proper fraction, with models.
description_plain: Your child can solve and explain single-step and multistep problems using addition and subtraction of fractions, and multiply whole numbers by proper fractions, sometimes using pictures or models.

subject: Math
description: Interpret the quotient and remainder when solving a contextual problem.
description_plain: Your child understands what the quotient and remainder mean when solving real-life division problems.

subject: Math
description: dividends do not exceed four digits.
description_plain: Your child works with division problems where the number being divided has up to four digits.

subject: Math
description: divisors do not exceed two digits; or
description_plain: Your child works with division problems where the number you divide by has up to two digits.

subject: Math
description: factors do not exceed two digits by three digits;
description_plain: Your child multiplies numbers where one factor has up to two digits and the other has up to three digits.

subject: Math
description: sums, differences, and products do not exceed five digits;
description_plain: Your child adds, subtracts, and multiplies numbers with answers up to five digits.

subject: Math
description: Represent, solve, and justify solutions to single-step and multistep contextual problems by applying strategies (e.g., estimation, properties of addition and multiplication) and algorithms, including the standard algorithm, involving addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division of whole numbers, with and without remainders, in which:
description_plain: Your child can solve and explain single-step and multistep real-life problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division of whole numbers, using strategies like estimation and standard methods.

subject: Math
description: Estimate the sum, difference, product, and quotient of whole numbers in contextual problems.
description_plain: Your child can estimate the answer when adding, subtracting, multiplying, or dividing whole numbers in real-life problems.

subject: Math
description: The student will estimate, represent, solve, and justify solutions to single-step and multistep contextual problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division with whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child can estimate, solve, and explain single-step and multistep real-life problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division with whole numbers.

subject: Math
description: Determine the prime factorization for a whole number up to 100.
description_plain: Your child can find the prime factors of any whole number up to 100.

subject: Math
description: Classify, compare, and contrast whole numbers up to 100 using the characteristics prime and composite.
description_plain: Your child can sort and compare whole numbers up to 100 by deciding if they are prime or composite numbers.

subject: Math
description: Given a whole number up to 100, create a concrete or pictorial representation to demonstrate whether the number is prime or composite, and justify reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can use pictures or objects to show if a number up to 100 is prime or composite and explain why.

subject: Math
description: The student will demonstrate an understanding of prime and composite numbers, and determine the prime factorization of a whole number up to 100.
description_plain: Your child understands what prime and composite numbers are and can find the prime factors of numbers up to 100.

subject: Math
description: Compare (using symbols <, >, =) and order (least to greatest and greatest to least) a set of no more than four decimals and fractions (proper, improper) and/or mixed numbers using multiple strategies (e.g., benchmarks, place value, number lines). Justify solutions orally, in writing, or with a model.*
description_plain: Your child can compare and order up to four decimals and fractions, using symbols like <, >, or =, and explain their answers with words or models.

subject: Math
description: Identify equivalent relationships between decimals and fractions with denominators that are thirds, eighths, and factors of 100 in their equivalent decimal form, with and without models.*
description_plain: Your child can recognize when decimals and fractions (with denominators like thirds, eighths, or factors of 100) are equal, sometimes using models.

subject: Math
description: Use concrete and pictorial models to represent decimals in their equivalent fraction form (thirds, eighths, and factors of 100).*
description_plain: Your child can use pictures or objects to show decimals as fractions with denominators like thirds, eighths, or factors of 100.

subject: Math
description: Use concrete and pictorial models to represent fractions with denominators that are thirds, eighths, and factors of 100 in their equivalent decimal form.*
description_plain: Your child can use pictures or objects to show fractions with denominators like thirds, eighths, or factors of 100 as decimals.

subject: Math
description: The student will use reasoning and justification to identify and represent equivalency between fractions (with denominators that are thirds, eighths, and factors of 100) and decimals; and compare and order sets of fractions (proper, improper, and/or mixed numbers having denominators of 12 or less) and decimals (through thousandths).
description_plain: Your child can explain and show how certain fractions and decimals are equal, and can compare and order fractions and decimals up to thousandths.

subject: Math
description: Interpret algebraic solutions in context to linear inequalities in one variable.
description_plain: Your child can explain what the solution to a one-variable linear inequality means in a real-world situation.

subject: Math
description: Identify a numerical value(s) that is part of the solution set of a given inequality.
description_plain: Your child can find numbers that make a given inequality true.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems in context that require the solution of a multistep linear inequality in one variable.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life problems that need several steps to solve an inequality with one variable.

subject: Math
description: Create a verbal situation in context given a multistep linear inequality in one variable.
description_plain: Your child can make up a real-life story that matches a multistep inequality with one variable.

subject: Math
description: Write multistep linear inequalities in one variable to represent a verbal situation, including those in context.
description_plain: Your child can write multistep inequalities with one variable to show a real-life situation.

subject: Math
description: Represent solutions to inequalities algebraically and graphically using a number line.
description_plain: Your child can show the answers to inequalities using math expressions and number lines.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of real numbers and properties of inequality to solve multistep linear inequalities (up to four steps) in one variable with the variable on one or both sides of the inequality. Coefficients and numeric terms will be rational. Inequalities may contain expressions that need to be expanded (using the distributive property) or require combining like terms to solve.
description_plain: Your child can use math rules to solve multistep inequalities with one variable, including expanding expressions and combining like terms.

subject: Math
description: The student will write and solve multistep linear inequalities in one variable, including problems in context that require the solution of a multistep linear inequality in one variable.
description_plain: Your child will write and solve multistep inequalities with one variable, including solving real-life problems.

subject: Math
description: Confirm algebraic solutions to linear equations in one variable.
description_plain: Your child can check if their answers to one-variable linear equations are correct.

subject: Math
description: Interpret algebraic solutions in context to linear equations in one variable.
description_plain: Your child can explain what the answers to linear equations mean in real-life situations.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems in context that require the solution of a multistep linear equation.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life problems that need several steps to solve a linear equation.

subject: Math
description: Create a verbal situation in context given a multistep linear equation in one variable.
description_plain: Your child can create a real-life story that matches a multistep linear equation with one variable.

subject: Math
description: Write a multistep linear equation in one variable to represent a verbal situation, including those in context.
description_plain: Your child can write multistep linear equations with one variable to represent real-life situations.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of real numbers and properties of equality to solve multistep linear equations in one variable (up to four steps). Coefficients and numeric terms will be rational. Equations may contain expressions that need to be expanded (using the distributive property) or require combining like terms to solve.
description_plain: Your child can use math rules to solve multistep linear equations with one variable, including expanding expressions and combining like terms.

subject: Math
description: Represent and solve multistep linear equations in one variable with the variable on one or both sides of the equation (up to four steps) using a variety of concrete materials and pictorial representations.
description_plain: Your child can use objects and pictures to show and solve multistep linear equations with one variable, even when the variable is on both sides.

subject: Math
description: The student will write and solve multistep linear equations in one variable, including problems in context that require the solution of a multistep linear equation in one variable.
description_plain: Your child will write and solve multistep linear equations with one variable, including solving real-life problems.

subject: Math
description: Create a context for a linear function given a graph, table, or equation in the form y = mx + b.
description_plain: Your child can make up a real-life story based on a graph, table, or equation of a linear function.

subject: Math
description: Write an equation of a linear function in the form y = mx + b, given a graph, table, or a situation in context.
description_plain: Your child can write the equation for a linear function using a graph, table, or real-life situation.

subject: Math
description: Create a table of values for a linear function given a graph, equation in the form of y = mx + b, or context.
description_plain: Your child can make a table of values for a linear function from a graph, equation, or real-life story.

subject: Math
description: Graph a linear function given a table, equation, or a situation in context.
description_plain: Your child can draw the graph of a linear function using a table, equation, or real-life example.

subject: Math
description: Describe key characteristics of linear functions including slope (m), y-intercept (b), and independent and dependent variables.
description_plain: Your child can explain important parts of linear functions like slope, y-intercept, and which values depend on others.

subject: Math
description: Determine how adding a constant (b) to the equation of a proportional relationship y = mx will translate the line on a graph.
description_plain: Your child understands how adding a number to the equation y = mx moves the line up or down on a graph.

subject: Math
description: The student will represent and solve problems, including those in context, by using linear functions and analyzing their key characteristics (the value of the y-intercept (b) and the coordinates of the ordered pairs in graphs will be limited to integers).
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life problems using straight-line equations and understand important parts like where the line crosses the y-axis and points on the graph, using only whole numbers.

subject: Math
description: Identify the domain and range of a function represented as a set of ordered pairs, a table, or a graph of discrete points.
description_plain: Your child can find the input values (domain) and output values (range) from a list of points, a table, or a graph made of separate points.

subject: Math
description: Determine whether a relation, represented by a set of ordered pairs, a table, or a graph of discrete points is a function. Sets are limited to no more than 10 ordered pairs.
description_plain: Your child can decide if a set of points, a table, or a graph shows a function, using up to 10 points.

subject: Math
description: The student will determine whether a given relation is a function and determine the domain and range of a function.
description_plain: Your child understands how to tell if a relation is a function and can find the input and output values for that function.

subject: Math
description: Simplify and generate equivalent algebraic expressions in one variable by applying the order of operations and properties of real numbers. Expressions may need to be expanded (using the distributive property) or require combining like terms to simplify. Expressions will include only linear and numeric terms. Coefficients and numeric terms may be rational.
description_plain: Your child can simplify and create algebra expressions with one variable by following math rules, including expanding expressions and combining like terms, using numbers that can be fractions.

subject: Math
description: Represent algebraic expressions using concrete manipulatives or pictorial representations (e.g., colored chips, algebra tiles), including expressions that apply the distributive property.
description_plain: Your child can show algebra expressions using objects or pictures, including expressions that use the distributive property.

subject: Math
description: The student will represent, simplify, and generate equivalent algebraic expressions in one variable.
description_plain: Your child can write, simplify, and create algebra expressions with one variable.

subject: Math
description: Sketch the line of best fit for data represented in a scatterplot.
description_plain: Your child can draw a line that best fits the points on a scatterplot.

subject: Math
description: Analyze and justify the relationship of the quantitative bivariate data represented in scatterplots.
description_plain: Your child can explain the relationship between two sets of numbers shown in a scatterplot.

subject: Math
description: Make observations about a set of data points in a scatterplot as having a positive linear relationship, a negative linear relationship, or no relationship
description_plain: Your child can look at points on a scatterplot and say if they go up together, go down together, or have no clear pattern.

subject: Math
description: Organize and represent numeric bivariate data using scatterplots with and without the use of technology.
description_plain: Your child can organize and show pairs of numbers using scatterplots, with or without technology.

subject: Math
description: Determine the data needed to answer a formulated question and collect the data (or acquire existing data) of no more than 20 items using various methods (e.g., observations, measurement, surveys, experiments).
description_plain: Your child can figure out what data is needed to answer a question and collect or find up to 20 pieces of data using different methods like surveys or measurements.

subject: Math
description: Formulate questions that require the collection or acquisition of data with a focus on scatterplots.
description_plain: Your child can come up with questions that need data collection, especially for making scatterplots.

subject: Math
description: The student will apply the data cycle (formulate questions; collect or acquire data; organize and represent data; and analyze data and communicate results) with a focus on scatterplots.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions, gather data, organize it, analyze it, and share the results, focusing on scatterplots.

subject: Math
description: Identify components of graphical displays that can be misleading.
description_plain: Your child can spot parts of graphs that might be confusing or misleading.

subject: Math
description: Given a contextual situation, justify which graphical representation (e.g., pictographs, bar graphs, line graphs, line plots/dot plots, stem-and-leaf plots, circle graphs, histograms, and boxplots) best represents the data.
description_plain: Your child can explain which type of graph (like bar, line, or circle graphs) best shows certain data.

subject: Math
description: Compare and analyze two data sets represented in boxplots.
description_plain: Your child can compare and understand two sets of data shown in boxplots.

subject: Math
description: Analyze data represented in a boxplot by making observations and drawing conclusions.
description_plain: Your child can look at a boxplot and make observations and conclusions about the data.

subject: Math
description: Describe how the presence of an extreme data point (outlier) affects the shape and spread of the data distribution of a boxplot.
description_plain: Your child understands how an unusual data point (outlier) changes the shape and spread of data in a boxplot.

subject: Math
description: Identify and describe the lower extreme (minimum), upper extreme (maximum), median, upper quartile, lower quartile, range, and interquartile range given a data set, represented by a boxplot.
description_plain: Your child can identify and describe key parts of a boxplot, like the smallest and largest values, middle value, quartiles, range, and the spread between quartiles.

subject: Math
description: Organize and represent a numeric data set of no more than 20 items, using boxplots, with and without the use of technology.
description_plain: Your child can organize up to 20 numbers and show them using boxplots, both by hand and with technology.

subject: Math
description: Determine how statistical bias might affect whether the data collected from the sample is representative of the larger population.
description_plain: Your child understands how bias can affect whether a small group of data truly represents a larger group.

subject: Math
description: Determine the data needed to answer a formulated question and collect the data (or acquire existing data) using various methods (e.g., observations, measurement, surveys, experiments).
description_plain: Your child can figure out what data is needed to answer a question and collect or find that data using ways like surveys or experiments.

subject: Math
description: Formulate questions that require the collection or acquisition of data with a focus on boxplots.
description_plain: Your child can create questions that need data collection, especially questions that can be answered using boxplots.

subject: Math
description: The student will apply the data cycle (formulate questions; collect or acquire data; organize and represent data; and analyze data and communicate results) with a focus on boxplots.
description_plain: Your child understands how to ask questions, gather data, organize it, and explain what it shows, focusing on boxplots.

subject: Math
description: Determine the probability of two dependent events.
description_plain: Your child can find the chance of two events happening when the first event affects the second.

subject: Math
description: Determine the probability of two independent events.
description_plain: Your child can find the chance of two events happening when the first event does not affect the second.

subject: Math
description: Compare and contrast the probability of independent and dependent events.
description_plain: Your child can compare how the chance of events changes when events depend on each other or not.

subject: Math
description: Determine whether two events are independent or dependent and explain how replacement impacts the probability.
description_plain: Your child can tell if two events affect each other and explain how putting things back changes the chance.

subject: Math
description: The student will use statistical investigation to determine the probability of independent and dependent events, including those in context.
description_plain: Your child can use data to figure out the chance of events happening, whether they affect each other or not.

subject: Math
description: Apply perimeter, circumference, and area formulas to solve contextual problems involving composite plane figures.
description_plain: Your child can use formulas for perimeter, circumference, and area to solve real-life problems with shapes made of several parts.

subject: Math
description: Subdivide a plane figure into triangles, rectangles, squares, trapezoids, parallelograms, and semicircles. Use the attributes of the subdivisions to determine the perimeter of the composite plane figure.
description_plain: Your child can break a shape into smaller shapes like triangles and rectangles to find the total perimeter.

subject: Math
description: Subdivide a plane figure into triangles, rectangles, squares, trapezoids, parallelograms, circles, and semicircles. Determine the area of subdivisions and combine to determine the area of the composite plane figure.
description_plain: Your child can break a shape into smaller parts like triangles and circles, find each area, and add them to get the total area.

subject: Math
description: The student will solve area and perimeter problems involving composite plane figures, including those in context.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems about the area and perimeter of shapes made from several smaller shapes, including real-world examples.

subject: Math
description: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem, and its converse, to solve problems involving right triangles in context.
description_plain: Your child can use the Pythagorean Theorem to solve problems about right triangles in real situations.

subject: Math
description: Determine the measure of a side of a right triangle, given the measures of the other two sides.
description_plain: Your child can find the length of a side in a right triangle when given the other two sides.

subject: Math
description: Identify the parts of a right triangle (the hypotenuse and the legs) given figures in various orientations.
description_plain: Your child can identify the longest side (hypotenuse) and the other sides (legs) of a right triangle, no matter how it is turned.

subject: Math
description: Determine whether a triangle is a right triangle given the measures of its three sides.
description_plain: Your child can decide if a triangle is a right triangle by looking at the lengths of its three sides.

subject: Math
description: Verify the Pythagorean Theorem using diagrams, concrete materials, and measurement.
description_plain: Your child can prove the Pythagorean Theorem using pictures, objects, and measuring.

subject: Math
description: The student will apply the Pythagorean Theorem to solve problems involving right triangles, including those in context.
description_plain: Your child can use the Pythagorean Theorem to solve problems about right triangles, including real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Identify and describe transformations in context (e.g., tiling, fabric, wallpaper designs, art).
description_plain: Your child can recognize and explain how shapes change in real-life examples like tiling, fabric, wallpaper, or art.

subject: Math
description: Sketch the image of a polygon that has been translated and reflected over the x- or y-axis, or reflected over the x- or y-axis and then translated.
description_plain: Your child can draw a shape after it has been moved and flipped over the x- or y-axis, or flipped and then moved.

subject: Math
description: Sketch the image of a polygon that has been reflected over the x- or y-axis.
description_plain: Your child can draw a shape after it has been flipped over the x- or y-axis.

subject: Math
description: Sketch the image of a polygon that has been translated vertically, horizontally, or a combination of both.
description_plain: Your child can draw a shape after it has been moved up, down, left, right, or both.

subject: Math
description: Given a preimage in the coordinate plane, identify the coordinates of the image of a polygon that has been translated and reflected over the x- or y-axis or reflected over the x- or y-axis and then translated.
description_plain: Your child can find the new coordinates of a shape after it has been moved and flipped over the x- or y-axis, or flipped and then moved.

subject: Math
description: Given a preimage in the coordinate plane, identify the coordinates of the image of a polygon that has been reflected over the x- or y-axis
description_plain: Your child can find the new coordinates of a shape after it has been flipped over the x- or y-axis.

subject: Math
description: Given a preimage in the coordinate plane, identify the coordinates of the image of a polygon that has been translated vertically, horizontally, or a combination of both.
description_plain: Your child can find the new coordinates of a shape after it has been moved up, down, left, right, or both.

subject: Math
description: The student will apply translations and reflections to polygons in the coordinate plane.
description_plain: Your child will practice moving and flipping shapes on a grid.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems in context involving volume of cones and square-based pyramids and the surface area of square-based pyramids.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life problems involving the volume of cones and square-based pyramids and the surface area of square-based pyramids.

subject: Math
description: Examine and explain the relationship between the volume of cones and cylinders, and the volume of rectangular prisms and square based pyramids.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the volume of cones relates to cylinders and how the volume of square-based pyramids relates to rectangular prisms.

subject: Math
description: Determine the volume of cones and square-based pyramids, using concrete objects, diagrams, and formulas.
description_plain: Your child can find the volume of cones and square-based pyramids using objects, pictures, and formulas.

subject: Math
description: Determine the surface area of square-based pyramids by using concrete objects, nets, diagrams, and formulas.
description_plain: Your child can find the surface area of square-based pyramids using objects, flat patterns, pictures, and formulas.

subject: Math
description: The student will investigate and determine the surface area of square-based pyramids and the volume of cones and square-based pyramids.
description_plain: Your child will learn how to find the surface area of square-based pyramids and the volume of cones and square-based pyramids.

subject: Math
description: Use the relationships among supplementary, complementary, vertical, and adjacent angles to solve problems, including those in context, involving the measure of unknown angles.
description_plain: Your child can use what they know about different angle pairs to solve problems and find missing angle measures.

subject: Math
description: Identify and describe the relationship between pairs of angles that are vertical, adjacent, supplementary, and complementary.
description_plain: Your child can identify and explain how pairs of angles are related, like vertical, adjacent, supplementary, and complementary angles.

subject: Math
description: Estimate and solve contextual problems that require the computation of the percent increase or decrease.
description_plain: Your child can estimate and solve real-world problems involving percent increases or decreases.

subject: Math
description: Estimate and solve contextual problems that require the computation of the sales tax, tip and resulting total.
description_plain: Your child can estimate and solve problems about sales tax, tips, and total amounts in everyday situations.

subject: Math
description: Estimate and solve contextual problems that require the computation of one discount or markup and the resulting sale price.
description_plain: Your child can estimate and solve problems about discounts or markups and find the final sale price.

subject: Math
description: The student will estimate and apply proportional reasoning and computational procedures to solve contextual problems.
description_plain: Your child will practice using proportions and calculations to solve real-life problems.

subject: Math
description: Describe each subset of the set of real numbers and include examples and non-examples.
description_plain: Your child understands different groups of real numbers and can give examples and non-examples of each.

subject: Math
description: Classify and explain why a given number is a member of a particular subset or subsets of the real number system.
description_plain: Your child can identify which groups of numbers (like whole numbers or fractions) a number belongs to and explain why.

subject: Math
description: Describe and illustrate the relationships among the subsets of the real number system by using representations (e.g., graphic organizers, number lines). Subsets include rational numbers, irrational numbers, integers, whole numbers, and natural numbers.
description_plain: Your child can show and explain how different types of numbers (like whole numbers, fractions, and decimals) are related using pictures or number lines.

subject: Math
description: The student will investigate and describe the relationship between the subsets of the real number system.
description_plain: Your child understands how different groups of numbers fit together and relate to each other.

subject: Math
description: Use multiple strategies (e.g., benchmarks, number line, equivalency) to compare and order no more than five real numbers expressed as integers, fractions (proper or improper), decimals, mixed numbers, percents, numbers written in scientific notation, radicals, and π. Radicals may include both positive and negative square roots of values from 0 to 400. Ordering may be in ascending or descending order. Justify solutions orally, in writing or with a model.
description_plain: Your child can compare and put in order up to five numbers written in different forms (like fractions, decimals, or percents) and explain their thinking clearly.

subject: Math
description: Use rational approximations (to the nearest hundredth) of irrational numbers to compare, order, and locate values on a number line. Radicals may include both positive and negative square roots of values from 0 to 400 yielding an irrational number.
description_plain: Your child can use rounded decimal values to compare, order, and place irrational numbers on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Estimate and identify the two consecutive natural numbers between which the positive square root of a given number lies and justify which natural number is the better approximation. Numbers are limited to natural numbers from 1 to 400.
description_plain: Your child can estimate which two whole numbers a square root falls between and explain which whole number is the closest guess.

subject: Math
description: The student will compare and order real numbers and determine the relationships between real numbers.
description_plain: Your child can compare and order different kinds of numbers and understand how they relate to each other.

subject: Math
description: Make conclusions based on the analysis of a set of bivariate data and communicate the results.
description_plain: Your child can look at two-variable data, draw conclusions from it, and explain the results.

description_plain: Your child can study how two sets of numbers relate to each other by looking at a scatterplot.

description: Investigate and explain the meaning of the rate of change (slope) and y-intercept (constant term) of a linear model in context.
description_plain: Your child can explain what the slope and starting point mean in a line graph that models real-life situations.

subject: Math
description: Investigate and explain the meaning of the rate of change (slope) and y-intercept (constant term) of a linear model in context.
description_plain: Your child can explain what the slope and starting point mean in a line graph that models real-life situations.

description_plain: Your child can explain what the slope and starting point mean in a line graph that models real-life situations.

description_plain: Your child can explain what the slope and starting point mean in a line graph that models real-life situations.

description_plain: Your child can use technology to find the best line or curve that fits data and talk about what the model does well and where it might not work perfectly.

description_plain: Your child can use technology to find the best line or curve that fits data and talk about what the model does well and where it might not work perfectly.
