description_plain: Your child can find and explain how one event causes another.

subject: Social Studies
description: comparing and contrasting historical, cultural, economic, and political perspectives;
description_plain: Your child compares different views from history, culture, economy, and politics.

subject: Social Studies
description: investigating and analyzing evidence from multiple sources to construct arguments;
description_plain: Your child looks at information from many sources to build a clear argument.

subject: Social Studies
description: developing questions, enhancing curiosity, and engaging in critical thinking and analysis;
description_plain: Your child asks questions and thinks carefully to understand topics better.

subject: Social Studies
description: applying geographic skills to determine and predict patterns and movement of people, places, and events;
description_plain: Your child uses map skills to find and guess patterns in where people and events happen.

subject: Social Studies
description: selecting and synthesizing evidence from information sources, including, but not limited to artifacts, primary and secondary sources, charts, graphs, diagrams, and geospatial technologies, including maps, GIS, and GPS imagery, to understand the regions of the world;
description_plain: Your child picks important facts from things like maps, charts, and artifacts to learn about world regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will demonstrate skills for historical thinking, geographical analysis, economic decision making, and responsible citizenship by
description_plain: Your child learns skills in history, geography, economics, and citizenship to understand the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing the contributions of artists and philosophers of the Italian Renaissance, including Leonardo da Vinci, Michelangelo, and Petrarch.
description_plain: Your child studies how artists and thinkers like Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo contributed to the Italian Renaissance.

subject: Social Studies
description: sequencing events related to the rise of Italian city-states and their political development, including Machiavelli’s theory of governing; and
description_plain: Your child puts in order the events that led to the rise of Italian city-states and learns about Machiavelli’s ideas on ruling.

subject: Social Studies
description: determining the economic, political, philosophical, and cultural foundations of the Italian Renaissance;
description_plain: Your child learns about the economic, political, philosophical, and cultural ideas that started the Italian Renaissance.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to understand the factors contributing to the European Renaissance by
description_plain: Your child uses history skills to understand what caused the European Renaissance.

subject: Social Studies
description: examining the roles of people in each society, including class structures, family life, warfare, religious beliefs and practices, and slavery.
description_plain: Your child studies the roles of people in societies, including their social classes, family life, wars, religion, and slavery.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the Meso-American achievements in astronomy and mathematics, including the development of the calendar and the Meso-American knowledge of seasonal changes to the civilizations’ agricultural systems; and
description_plain: Your child learns about Meso-American achievements in astronomy and math, like their calendar and how they used seasons for farming.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the artistic and oral traditions and architecture in the three civilizations;
description_plain: Your child can talk about the art, stories, and buildings from the Mayan, Aztec, and Incan civilizations.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining how and where each empire arose and how the Aztec and Incan empires were defeated by the Spanish;
description_plain: Your child understands where and how the Aztec, Incan, and other empires started and how the Spanish defeated the Aztec and Incan empires.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the locations, landforms, and climates of Mexico, Central America, and South America and their effects on Mayan, Aztec, and Incan economies, trade, and the development of urban societies;
description_plain: Your child can describe the land, climate, and places in Mexico, Central, and South America and explain how these affected the economies, trade, and cities of the Mayan, Aztec, and Incan people.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to understand the Meso-American and Andean civilizations by
description_plain: Your child will use history skills to learn about the Meso-American and Andean civilizations.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining the importance of the Catholic church as a political and intellectual institution and its effects on education, religious orders, preservation of languages and texts, and philosophy.
description_plain: Your child understands why the Catholic Church was important in politics and education and how it helped keep languages, books, and ideas alive.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the decline of Muslim rule in the Iberian Peninsula that resulted in the expansion of Christian rule and the rise of Spanish and Portuguese kingdoms; and
description_plain: Your child can explain how Muslim rule ended in the Iberian Peninsula and how Christian kingdoms in Spain and Portugal grew.

subject: Social Studies
description: tracing the causes and course of the Crusades and the effects on the Christian, Muslim, and Jewish populations in Europe and territorial claims;
description_plain: Your child can follow the causes and events of the Crusades and their effects on Christians, Muslims, and Jews in Europe and land ownership.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing the reasons for the Great Schism in 1054;
description_plain: Your child can explain why the Christian Church split into two parts in 1054.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining the significance of developments in medieval English legal and constitutional practices, including, but not limited to the Magna Carta, parliaments, development of habeas corpus, and independent judiciary in England;
description_plain: Your child understands important changes in medieval English laws and government, like the Magna Carta and the idea of fair courts.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to analyze the conflict and cooperation between the Papacy and European monarchs by
description_plain: Your child will use history skills to study the conflicts and cooperation between the Pope and European kings.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the growth of towns and trade as Europe emerged from feudalism.
description_plain: Your child can describe how towns and trade grew as Europe moved away from feudalism.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining the development and role of feudalism in the medieval European economy and the role of the manor as the center of feudal relationships at the foundation of the political order; and
description_plain: Your child understands how feudalism worked in medieval Europe and how manors were the center of this system.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the spread of Christianity north of the Alps and the roles played by the early church and by monasteries in its diffusion after the fall of the western half of the Roman Empire; and
description_plain: Your child can describe how Christianity spread north of the Alps and the role of the early church and monasteries after the Roman Empire fell.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the geography of the European and Eurasian landmass, including location, topography, waterways, vegetation, and climate, and their relationship to ways of life in Medieval Europe;
description_plain: Your child can describe Europe's geography and explain how land, rivers, plants, and climate affected life in medieval Europe.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to understand the civilizations of Medieval Europe by
description_plain: Your child will use history skills to learn about the civilizations of Medieval Europe.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing the rise of a military society in the late 12th century and the role of the samurai in that society.
description_plain: Your child can explain how a military society grew in Japan in the late 1100s and the role of the samurai.

subject: Social Studies
description: examining the ninth and tenth centuries’ golden age of literature, art, and drama and its lasting effects on culture today, including Murasaki Shikibu’s Tale of Genji; and
description_plain: Your child can explore the golden age of Japanese literature, art, and drama in the 900s and 1000s, including the Tale of Genji.

subject: Social Studies
description: tracing the development of distinctive forms of Japanese Buddhism;
description_plain: Your child can trace how different types of Japanese Buddhism developed.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the values, social customs, and traditions prescribed by the lord-vassal system consisting of shogun, daimyo, and samurai and the lasting influence of the warrior code in the 20th century;
description_plain: Your child understands the social rules and traditions of the Japanese lord-vassal system with shogun, daimyo, and samurai, and how the warrior code influenced later times.

subject: Social Studies
description: discussing the reign of Prince Shotoku of Japan and the characteristics of Japanese society and family life during his reign;
description_plain: Your child can talk about Prince Shotoku's reign in Japan and what family and society were like during that time.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the significance of Japan’s proximity to China and Korea and the intellectual, linguistic, religious, and philosophical influence of those countries on Japan;
description_plain: Your child can explain how Japan’s closeness to China and Korea affected its language, religion, ideas, and culture.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to understand the civilizations of Medieval Japan by
description_plain: Your child will use history skills to learn about the civilizations of Medieval Japan.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the importance of written and oral traditions in the transmission of African history and culture.
description_plain: Your child understands how African history and culture were passed down through stories and writing.

subject: Social Studies
description: tracing the growth of the Arabic language in government, trade, and Islam; and
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Arabic language grew in government, trade, and religion.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the role of the trans-Saharan caravan trade in the changing religious and cultural characteristics of West Africa and the influence of Islam;
description_plain: Your child describes how trade across the Sahara Desert changed West Africa’s religion and culture, especially with the spread of Islam.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing the importance of family, labor specialization, and regional commerce in the development of states and cities in West Africa;
description_plain: Your child can explain how families, job skills, and local trade helped build cities and states in West Africa.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the Niger River and the relationship of vegetation zones of forest, savannah, and desert to trade in gold, salt, food, and enslaved people, as well as the growth of the Ghana and Mali empires;
description_plain: Your child describes the Niger River and how different land areas affected trade in gold, salt, food, and slaves, and how this helped Ghana and Mali grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to describe the sub-Saharan civilizations of Ghana and Mali in Medieval Africa by
description_plain: Your child will use history skills to learn about the Ghana and Mali civilizations in Medieval Africa.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the development of the imperial state and the scholar-official class.
description_plain: Your child can describe how China’s imperial government and scholar-officials developed.

subject: Social Studies
description: tracing the historic influence of the tea trade, the manufacture of paper, woodblock printing, the compass, and the invention of gunpowder; and
description_plain: Your child can explain how tea trade, paper making, printing, the compass, and gunpowder influenced history.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining the importance of overland trade and maritime expeditions between China and other civilizations in the Mongol Ascendancy and Ming Dynasty;
description_plain: Your child understands why land and sea trade between China and other places were important during the Mongol and Ming times.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing the influences of Confucianism and changes in Confucian thought during the Sung and Mongol periods;
description_plain: Your child can explain how Confucian ideas changed during the Sung and Mongol periods.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing agricultural, technological, and commercial developments during the Tang and Sung periods;
description_plain: Your child describes farming, technology, and trade improvements during the Tang and Sung dynasties.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the reunification of China under the Tang Dynasty and reasons for the spread of Buddhism in Tang China, Korea, and Japan;
description_plain: Your child can explain how China was reunited under the Tang Dynasty and why Buddhism spread in China, Korea, and Japan.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to understand the civilizations of China in the Middle Ages by
description_plain: Your child will use history skills to learn about China’s civilizations in the Middle Ages.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the growth of cities and the role of merchants in Muslim society and the expansion of trade routes in Asia, Africa, Europe, and the Indian Ocean and identifying the products and inventions that traveled along these routes, including spices, textiles, paper, steel, and new crops.
description_plain: Your child describes how cities grew, merchants helped trade, and how goods like spices, cloth, paper, and steel moved across Asia, Africa, Europe, and the Indian Ocean.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the expansion of territory under Muslim rule, the spread of Islam and the Arabic language among people in these territories, and the cultural and religious acceptance of Islam and the Arabic language; and
description_plain: Your child can explain how Muslim rule expanded, spreading Islam and the Arabic language, and how people accepted these changes.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining the significance of the Qur’an and the Sunnah as the primary sources of Islamic beliefs, practice, and law, and their influence in Muslims’ daily life;
description_plain: Your child understands how the Qur’an and Sunnah guide Muslim beliefs, laws, and daily life.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the origins, beliefs, traditions, customs, persecution, and spread of Islam;
description_plain: Your child can describe where Islam started, its beliefs, customs, challenges, and how it spread.

subject: Social Studies
description: identifying the physical features and describing the relationship between climate, land and surrounding bodies and water, and nomadic and sedentary ways of life of the Arabian peninsula;
description_plain: Your child identifies the land and climate of the Arabian Peninsula and explains how these affected the lives of nomads and settled people.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to understand Islamic societies by
description_plain: Your child will use history and social studies skills to learn about Islamic societies.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining the influence of Rome, including, but not limited to citizenship, slavery, Roman law and guaranteed rights, Roman art, architecture, engineering, and philosophy.
description_plain: Your child can explain how Rome influenced citizenship, slavery, laws, rights, art, buildings, engineering, and philosophy.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the origins, beliefs, customs, and spread of Christianity, including the persecution and eventual adoption and transmission of Christianity and the New Testament, differences between the Eastern and Western churches, and the influence of Christianity throughout Europe, Middle Asia, the Middle East, and North Africa; and
description_plain: Your child will describe where Christianity began, its beliefs and customs, how it spread, and its impact on Europe, Asia, and Africa.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the social, cultural, political, and economic development of the Byzantine Empire, including, but not limited to the establishment of Constantinople, and the eventual division of the Roman Empire;
description_plain: Your child can describe how the Byzantine Empire grew socially, culturally, politically, and economically, including the founding of Constantinople and the split of the Roman Empire.

subject: Social Studies
description: comparing and contrasting the political, social, and religious structure and development of the Roman Republic and the Roman Empire;
description_plain: Your child will compare how the Roman Republic and Roman Empire were different in government, society, and religion.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the influence of geography on Rome’s development and the factors that threatened territorial cohesion;
description_plain: Your child can explain how geography affected Rome’s growth and what challenges threatened its land.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to understand Rome and the Byzantine Empire by
description_plain: Your child will use history and social studies skills to learn about Rome and the Byzantine Empire.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining the influence of ancient Greek contributions, including, but not limited to science, art, architecture, philosophy, and mathematics in the present day.
description_plain: Your child can explain how ancient Greece’s science, art, buildings, philosophy, and math still affect us today.

subject: Social Studies
description: evaluating the significance of Alexander the Great’s conquest of Greece and the formation and the spread of Hellenistic culture; and
description_plain: Your child will understand why Alexander the Great’s conquests were important and how they spread Greek culture.

subject: Social Studies
description: evaluating the causes and consequences of the Persian and Peloponnesian wars;
description_plain: Your child can explain the causes and effects of the Persian and Peloponnesian wars.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the social, cultural, political, and economic development of Greece, including, but not limited to the significance of Athens and Sparta, the development of citizenship, and the different forms of democracy;
description_plain: Your child will describe how Greece developed socially, culturally, politically, and economically, including Athens, Sparta, citizenship, and democracy.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the social, cultural, political, and economic aspects of ancient Persia;
description_plain: Your child can describe the social, cultural, political, and economic life of ancient Persia.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the major geographic features of the region and analyzing the effect that geography had on its development;
description_plain: Your child will describe the main geographic features of Persia and explain how geography affected its growth.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to understand Persia and Greece by
description_plain: Your child will use history and social studies skills to learn about Persia and Greece.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the influence of Confucianism, Taoism, and Legalism.
description_plain: Your child can describe the ideas and influence of Confucianism, Taoism, and Legalism.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the social, cultural, political, and economic development of ancient China; and
description_plain: Your child will describe how ancient China developed socially, culturally, politically, and economically.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the origins, beliefs, customs, and spread of Buddhism;
description_plain: Your child can describe where Buddhism started, its beliefs and customs, and how it spread.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the origins, beliefs, customs, and spread of Hinduism;
description_plain: Your child will describe where Hinduism began, its beliefs and customs, and how it spread.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the social, cultural, political, and economic characteristics that define the societies of the Indian subcontinent, including, but not limited to contributions and the concepts of Varna and Jati;
description_plain: Your child can describe the social, cultural, political, and economic features of ancient Indian societies, including their contributions and social classes like Varna and Jati.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing the impact of geography on the development of ancient India and China, including locating them in time and place and describing their major geographic features;
description_plain: Your child will explain how geography affected the growth of ancient India and China, including where and when they existed and their main geographic features.

subject: Social Studies
description: The students will apply history and social science skills to describe ancient Asian societies by
description_plain: Your child can use history skills to explain what life was like in ancient Asian societies.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the development of the Phoenicians.
description_plain: Your child will describe how the Phoenician civilization developed.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the development of the Israelites and the origins, beliefs, traditions, customs, persecution, and spread of Judaism; and
description_plain: Your child will explain how the Israelites developed and learn about the beginnings, beliefs, traditions, challenges, and spread of Judaism.

subject: Social Studies
description: locating and explaining the development of Mesopotamia;
description_plain: Your child can find Mesopotamia on a map and explain how it developed.

subject: Social Studies
description: locating and explaining the development of Egypt and Nubia;
description_plain: Your child can locate Egypt and Nubia on a map and explain how these civilizations grew.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to describe early societies in the Fertile Crescent by
description_plain: Your child will use history skills to describe early societies in the Fertile Crescent region.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing how archaeological discoveries change current understanding of early societies.
description_plain: Your child understands how new archaeological finds can change what we know about early societies.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing how technological and social developments gave rise to sedentary settlements; and
description_plain: Your child can explain how new tools and social changes helped people settle in one place.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing characteristics of hunter-gatherer societies, including their use of tools and fire;
description_plain: Your child will describe hunter-gatherer societies, including how they used tools and fire.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining the effect that geography had on the emergence and migration of hunter-gatherer societies;
description_plain: Your child can explain how geography affected where hunter-gatherer groups lived and moved.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the archaeological evidence of the first human and their geographic locations;
description_plain: Your child will describe the earliest humans and where their remains have been found.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to describe the period from the Paleolithic Era into the Neolithic Era by
description_plain: Your child will use history skills to describe how people lived from the Paleolithic Era to the Neolithic Era.

subject: Social Studies
description: contextualizing and corroborating sources to evaluate sources for credibility, propaganda, and bias to determine patterns and trends to understand the ancient world.
description_plain: Your child can check different sources to see if they are trustworthy and understand the ancient world better.

subject: Social Studies
description: developing products that reflect an understanding of research and content; and
description_plain: Your child will create projects that show they understand their research and what they learned.

subject: Social Studies
description: engaging and communicating as a civil and informed individual with different perspectives;
description_plain: Your child will communicate respectfully with others who have different opinions.

subject: Social Studies
description: using economic decision-making models to analyze and explain the incentives for and consequences of a specific choice;
description_plain: Your child can use simple economic ideas to explain why people make certain choices and what happens because of them.

subject: Social Studies
description: determining cause and effect to analyze connections;
description_plain: Your child can find cause-and-effect relationships to understand how events are connected.

subject: Social Studies
description: comparing and contrasting historical, cultural, economic, and political perspectives;
description_plain: Your child will compare different historical, cultural, economic, and political viewpoints.

subject: Social Studies
description: investigating and analyzing evidence from multiple sources to construct arguments and draw conclusions;
description_plain: Your child can look at evidence from many sources to build arguments and make conclusions.

subject: Social Studies
description: questioning to construct arguments, using evidence from multiple sources;
description_plain: Your child will ask questions and use evidence from different sources to support their ideas.

subject: Social Studies
description: applying geographic skills to determine and predict patterns and trends of people, places, and events;
description_plain: Your child can use map skills to find and guess patterns about where people live and what events happen.

subject: Social Studies
description: selecting and synthesizing evidence from information sources, including, but not limited to artifacts, primary and secondary sources, charts, graphs, and diagrams related to events in world history;
description_plain: Your child can pick out important facts from different sources like old objects, documents, charts, and pictures about world history events.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to the content by
description_plain: Your child understands how to use history and social studies skills to learn about the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing economic interdependence, including the rise of multinational corporations, international organizations, and trade agreements.
description_plain: Your child can explain how countries depend on each other through big companies, groups, and trade deals.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing the increasing impact, events, and conditions that have given rise to international terrorism, including, but not limited to the U.S. Embassy bombing in Beirut, the Lockerbie Bombing of Pan AM Flight 103, the U.S. Embassy bombings in Nairobi and Kenya, and the 2011 Breivik shootings; and
description_plain: Your child can look at events and reasons behind international terrorism, including specific attacks like the U.S. Embassy bombings and the 2011 Breivik shootings.

subject: Social Studies
description: examine the development, role, and effects of technology, including social media and chemical and biological technologies;
description_plain: Your child can explore how technology, including social media and chemical or biological tools, has developed and affected the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: identifying contemporary economic and political issues and ethnic and religious conflicts resulting in the migrations of refugees;
description_plain: Your child can identify current economic and political problems and conflicts that cause people to become refugees.

subject: Social Studies
description: identifying modern era genocides and crimes against humanity, including, but not limited to Mao’s Cultural Revolution, the Stalin regime, Armenia, Cambodia, Fidel Castro’s Cuba, Darfur, Rwanda, and China’s minority Uyghur population;
description_plain: Your child can recognize modern genocides and crimes against humanity, like those during Mao’s Cultural Revolution and in Rwanda.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to explain global changes during the 21st century by
description_plain: Your child understands how to use history and social studies skills to explain changes around the world in the 21st century.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining the effects of decolonization and other methods of gaining independence.
description_plain: Your child can explain how countries gained independence through decolonization and other ways.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the end of the League of Nations’ mandate system and the creation of states in the Middle East, including the roles of Golda Meir and Gamal Abdel Nasser; and
description_plain: Your child can describe how the League of Nations’ control ended and new countries formed in the Middle East, including leaders like Golda Meir and Gamal Abdel Nasser.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing African independence movements in Ghana, Algeria, Kenya, and South Africa, including, but not limited to Jomo Kenyatta’s leadership of Kenya and Nelson Mandela’s role in South Africa;
description_plain: Your child can describe independence movements in African countries like Ghana, Algeria, Kenya, and South Africa, including leaders like Jomo Kenyatta and Nelson Mandela.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the struggles for self-rule, including Gandhi’s leadership and the development of India’s democracy;
description_plain: Your child can describe the fight for self-rule in India, including Gandhi’s leadership and how India became a democracy.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to identify the political, economic, and socioeconomic aspects of independence movements and decolonization by
description_plain: Your child understands how to use history and social studies skills to learn about independence movements and decolonization.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing how nations around the world developed a culture of global interdependence.
description_plain: Your child can analyze how countries around the world became connected and dependent on each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: examining the political and economic causes and global consequences of the breakup of the Soviet Union; and
description_plain: Your child can examine the political and economic reasons for the Soviet Union breaking apart and what happened worldwide because of it.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining the collapse of communism in the Soviet Union and Eastern Europe and the end of the Cold War, including the actions of Mikhail Gorbachev, Pope John Paul II, Ronald Reagan, Margaret Thatcher, and Vaclav Havel;
description_plain: Your child can explain how communism ended in the Soviet Union and Eastern Europe and how the Cold War finished, including the roles of leaders like Gorbachev and Reagan.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing conflicts, events, and major leaders in Asia, including Mao Zedong, Chiang Kaishek, Deng Xiaoping, and Ho Chi Minh, and Tiananmen Square;
description_plain: Your child can describe important conflicts and leaders in Asia, like Mao Zedong and Ho Chi Minh, and events such as Tiananmen Square.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the events, conflicts, and revolutionary movements, including, but not limited to the Berlin Blockade, the Suez Canal Crisis, the Hungarian Revolution, the Bay of Pigs, the Cuban Missile Crisis, and the Prague Spring, as well as the impact of clandestine operations on the Cold War;
description_plain: Your child can describe key Cold War events and conflicts like the Berlin Blockade and Cuban Missile Crisis, and how secret operations affected the Cold War.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining the causes, the domino theory, the role of containment, and the differences between the United States and Soviet Union economic and political systems;
description_plain: Your child can explain the causes of the Cold War, the idea of the domino theory, the U.S. strategy of containment, and differences between the U.S. and Soviet systems.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to understand the significance of the Cold War during the second half of the 20th century by
description_plain: Your child will learn why the Cold War was important during the second half of the 20th century.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the heroic aspects, including, but not limited to D-Day, the French Resistance, the Dunkirk Evacuation, covert action, and Operation Jedburgh.
description_plain: Your child can describe brave actions during World War II, like D-Day, the French Resistance, the Dunkirk Evacuation, secret missions, and Operation Jedburgh.

subject: Social Studies
description: examining the effects of the war, with emphasis on the terms of the peace, the war crimes trials, the division of Europe, plans to rebuild Germany and Japan, the creation of international cooperative organizations, the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948), and the creation and defense of the modern state of Israel; and
description_plain: Your child will look at what happened after World War II, including peace agreements, war crime trials, how Europe was divided, rebuilding Germany and Japan, creating international groups, the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, and the start of modern Israel.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing key causes, events, victims, and impacts of the Holocaust, including antisemitism, the rise of the Nazi Party, Kristallnacht, the establishment of ghettos, concentration and death camps, mobile killing squads, rescue, Jewish resistance, and liberation;
description_plain: Your child understands the causes and effects of the Holocaust, including antisemitism, the rise of the Nazis, Kristallnacht, ghettos, concentration camps, killing squads, rescue efforts, Jewish resistance, and liberation.

subject: Social Studies
description: identifying the role of technology in the war, including, but not limited to naval power, cavity magnetron and radar, computers (i.e., the Electronic Numerical Integrator and Computer [ENIAC]), antibiotics, and the atomic bomb;
description_plain: Your child can identify important World War II technologies like naval power, radar, early computers, antibiotics, and the atomic bomb.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the major battles, including, but not limited to Leningrad, Midway, Normandy, Okinawa, and Stalingrad;
description_plain: Your child can describe major World War II battles such as Leningrad, Midway, Normandy, Okinawa, and Stalingrad.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining economic and political causes and identifying major events and leaders of the war, including, but not limited to Winston Churchill, Dwight Eisenhower, Adolf Hitler, Michinomiya Hirohito, Joseph Stalin, Douglas MacArthur, Franklin D. Roosevelt, Harry Truman, and Admiral Chester Nimitz;
description_plain: Your child will explain the economic and political causes of World War II and learn about key events and leaders like Churchill, Eisenhower, Hitler, Hirohito, Stalin, MacArthur, Roosevelt, Truman, and Nimitz.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to understand World War II by
description_plain: Your child will use history skills to understand the causes, events, and effects of World War II.

subject: Social Studies
description: examining the rise of totalitarianism.
description_plain: Your child will learn about how totalitarian governments rose to power.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining the causes and effects of worldwide depression in the 1930s; and
description_plain: Your child will explain what caused the worldwide depression in the 1930s and how it affected people.

subject: Social Studies
description: identifying the causes and consequences of the Russian Revolution;
description_plain: Your child will identify what caused the Russian Revolution and what happened because of it.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing and explaining the terms of the Treaty of Versailles, the actions of the League of Nations, and the mandate system;
description_plain: Your child will analyze the Treaty of Versailles, the League of Nations, and the mandate system after World War I.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing major battles, including, but not limited to Gallipoli, Marne, Meuse-Argonne, Somme, and Verdun;
description_plain: Your child can describe important World War I battles like Gallipoli, Marne, Meuse-Argonne, Somme, and Verdun.

subject: Social Studies
description: identifying the changes to modern warfare exemplified in battles along the Eastern and Western fronts;
description_plain: Your child will identify how modern warfare changed during battles on the Eastern and Western fronts in World War I.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining economic and political causes and identifying major events and leaders of the war, including, but not limited to the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand, Georges Clemenceau, John J. Pershing, Kaiser Friedrich Wilhelm II, and Woodrow Wilson;
description_plain: Your child will explain the economic and political causes of World War I and learn about major events and leaders like the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand, Clemenceau, Pershing, Kaiser Wilhelm II, and Wilson.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to understand World War I by
description_plain: Your child will use history skills to understand the causes, events, and effects of World War I.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining the events related to the unification of Germany and the role of Bismarck.
description_plain: Your child will explain how Germany became united and the role of Bismarck in that process.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining the events related to the unification of Italy and the role of Italian nationalism; and
description_plain: Your child will explain how Italy became united and how Italian nationalism helped.

subject: Social Studies
description: evaluating responses to imperialism, including, but not limited to the Sepoy Mutiny and the Boxer Rebellion;
description_plain: Your child will evaluate how people responded to imperialism, including events like the Sepoy Mutiny and the Boxer Rebellion.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing the effects of the First and Second Industrial Revolutions;
description_plain: Your child will analyze how the First and Second Industrial Revolutions changed society and the economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining the roles of resources, capital, and entrepreneurship in developing an industrial economy;
description_plain: Your child can explain how resources, money, and business ideas helped build factories and industries.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to analyze the global impact of changes in European nations between 1800 and 1900 by
description_plain: Your child will use history skills to study how changes in European countries between 1800 and 1900 affected the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: identifying trading partners, resources, and products exchanged with major Central and Southern African empires.
description_plain: Your child can name trading partners, resources, and goods traded by major Central and Southern African empires.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing the adoption of African Christianity in Kongo and comparing it to the practice of Indigenous religions in the Zulu Empire; and
description_plain: Your child can compare how Christianity was adopted in Kongo with how Indigenous religions were practiced in the Zulu Empire.

subject: Social Studies
description: comparing and contrasting the development of Central and Southern Africa, including, but not limited to the political systems of the Songhai, Asante (Ashanti), Kongo, and Zulu empires;
description_plain: Your child can compare how Central and Southern African empires like Songhai, Asante, Kongo, and Zulu developed, especially their governments.

subject: Social Studies
description: examining the Swahili trade network and its impacts on Eastern Africa;
description_plain: Your child understands how the Swahili trade network worked and how it affected Eastern Africa.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing the role of the Ashanti and other powerful Western African empires in the Transatlantic Slave Trade;
description_plain: Your child can explain the role of the Ashanti and other West African empires in the Transatlantic Slave Trade.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing the role of religion in Eastern and Western Africa, including Islam in Songhai, Coptic Christianity in Ethiopia, and Animism in the Songhai and Asante (Ashanti) Empires;
description_plain: Your child can describe how religion influenced Eastern and Western Africa, including Islam, Coptic Christianity, and Animism.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining the influence of Askia Muhammad in the region;
description_plain: Your child can explain how Askia Muhammad influenced the region.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the location and development of Eastern and Western Africa;
description_plain: Your child can describe where Eastern and Western Africa are and how they developed.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to understand sub-Saharan Africa from approximately 1500 A.D. to approximately 1800 A.D. by
description_plain: Your child will study the history and culture of sub-Saharan Africa from about 1500 to 1800 A.D.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the location, origins, and development of Japan, including, but not limited to the incentives and consequences of the Tokugawa shogunate’s closed-country policy, the roles of important figures in Japanese society, such as the Emperor and the Shogun, and the importance of religion in Japanese society.
description_plain: Your child can describe where Japan is, how it developed, and important things like the Tokugawa shogunate’s closed-country policy, the roles of the Emperor and Shogun, and religion.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the location, origins, and development of China, including, but not limited to the expansion, development, and social and cultural patterns within the Ming and Qing (Manchu) dynasties; and
description_plain: Your child can describe where China is and how it grew during the Ming and Qing dynasties, including social and cultural changes.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the location and development of northern and southern empires in India, including, but not limited to the major trading posts, the growth of Sikhism challenging the Mughal Empire, and cultural developments;
description_plain: Your child can describe where northern and southern empires in India were, their trade, the rise of Sikhism, and cultural growth.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the location and development of previously established trade routes, the economic success, the influence of religion, and the factors contributing to the longevity of the Ottoman Empire’s influence and power;
description_plain: Your child can describe where the Ottoman Empire was, its trade routes, economy, religion, and why it stayed powerful for so long.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to understand Asia from approximately 1500 A.D. to approximately 1800 A.D. by
description_plain: Your child will study the history and culture of Asia from about 1500 to 1800 A.D.

subject: Social Studies
description: assessing the effect of Napoleon and the Congress of Vienna on political power in Europe.
description_plain: Your child can explain how Napoleon and the Congress of Vienna changed political power in Europe.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining the influence of the American Revolution on the causes and effects of the French and Latin American Revolutions; and
description_plain: Your child can explain how the American Revolution influenced the causes and results of the French and Latin American Revolutions.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the development of constitutional monarchy in Great Britain, with emphasis on the English Civil War and the Glorious Revolution and their impacts on democracy;
description_plain: Your child can describe how Great Britain developed a constitutional monarchy, focusing on the English Civil War and the Glorious Revolution and their effects on democracy.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the Age of Absolutism with emphasis on the development of France and Louis XIV and the Hapsburg empire and Charles V;
description_plain: Your child can describe the Age of Absolutism by learning about France under Louis XIV and the Hapsburg empire under Charles V.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing Enlightenment themes and how they influenced the political foundations of Virginia and the United States;
description_plain: Your child can explain how ideas from the Enlightenment influenced the government and laws in Virginia and the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: defining and describing how the Scientific Revolution and Enlightenment influenced the European view of the world, including, but not limited to Descartes, Hume, Kant, Locke, Montesquieu, Isaac Newton, Rousseau, and Voltaire;
description_plain: Your child understands how the Scientific Revolution and Enlightenment changed how Europeans saw the world, including the ideas of important thinkers like Descartes and Newton.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the series of wars in Europe, including, but not limited to the French Wars of Religion, the Thirty Years War, the German Peasants’ War, the Tudor Rebellions, and the Dutch Revolt;
description_plain: Your child can describe several important wars in Europe, such as the French Wars of Religion and the Thirty Years War.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to analyze the political, socio-cultural, geographic, religious, and economic conditions in Europe, Russia, and the Americas that led to political unrest and revolution from approximately 1500 (A.D.) to about 1800 by
description_plain: Your child will study the political, cultural, religious, and economic reasons that caused unrest and revolutions in Europe, Russia, and the Americas between 1500 and 1800.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing how competition for colonies among Britain, France, and Spain changed the economic system of Europe.
description_plain: Your child can explain how competition for colonies among Britain, France, and Spain changed Europe's economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: comparing and contrasting the social, political, economic, and cultural effects of European colonization and the responses of Indigenous peoples in Africa, Asia, and the Americas; and
description_plain: Your child compares how European colonization affected societies and how Indigenous peoples in Africa, Asia, and the Americas responded.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining the political, social, cultural, and economic goals of European exploration and colonization;
description_plain: Your child explains the political, social, cultural, and economic reasons why Europeans explored and settled new lands.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to describe European exploration by
description_plain: Your child will describe European exploration and its effects using history and social science skills.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the effect of religious conflicts on society and government actions, including, but not limited to the Inquisition and the Catholic Reformation.
description_plain: Your child describes how religious conflicts, like the Inquisition and Catholic Reformation, affected society and government actions.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing how the Renaissance and Reformation led to changing cultural values, traditions, and philosophies and the role of the printing press in disseminating these changes; and
description_plain: Your child explains how the Renaissance and Reformation changed culture and ideas, and how the printing press helped spread these changes.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining the effects of the theological, political, and economic differences that emerged, including the views and actions of Martin Luther, John Calvin, Henry VIII, and Elizabeth I;
description_plain: Your child explains how religious and political differences, including the actions of Martin Luther and Henry VIII, affected Europe.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to analyze the Renaissance and Protestant Reformation in terms of their impacts on Western civilization by
description_plain: Your child will study how the Renaissance and Protestant Reformation impacted Western civilization.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing major trade patterns, regional and global interactions, cultural exchanges, technological, and scientific exchanges.
description_plain: Your child analyzes major trade routes and how cultures, technology, and science were shared around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the beliefs, sacred writings, traditions, customs, and growth of major religions, including, but not limited to Buddhism, Christianity, Hinduism, Islam, Judaism, and Sikhism; and
description_plain: Your child describes the beliefs, sacred writings, traditions, and growth of major religions like Buddhism, Christianity, and Islam.

subject: Social Studies
description: locating major states and empires;
description_plain: Your child can find and locate major states and empires on a map.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to analyze the political, cultural, geographic, and economic conditions in the world around 1500 A.D. by
description_plain: Your child will study the political, cultural, geographic, and economic conditions around the world in the year 1500.

subject: Social Studies
description: contextualizing and corroborating sources for credibility, propaganda, and bias to determine patterns and trends to understand the modern world.
description_plain: Your child learns to check if sources are trustworthy or biased to understand patterns in the modern world.

subject: Social Studies
description: developing products that reflect an understanding of research and content to make real-life connections; and
description_plain: Your child creates projects that show they understand their research and can connect it to real life.

subject: Social Studies
description: engaging and communicating as an informed individual with different perspectives;
description_plain: Your child practices sharing ideas and listening to different points of view.

subject: Social Studies
description: using decision-making models, including, but not limited to T-charts and Venn diagrams, to analyze and explain the incentives for and consequences of a specific choice;
description_plain: Your child uses tools like T-charts and Venn diagrams to think about choices and their reasons and results.

subject: Social Studies
description: determining cause and effect to analyze connections;
description_plain: Your child can figure out what caused events and what effects they had to understand how things are connected.

subject: Social Studies
description: comparing and contrasting historical, cultural, economic, and political perspectives;
description_plain: Your child can compare different viewpoints about history, culture, economy, and politics to see how they are alike and different.

subject: Social Studies
description: investigating and analyzing evidence from multiple sources to construct arguments and draw conclusions;
description_plain: Your child can look at information from many sources to build ideas and make conclusions.

subject: Social Studies
description: questioning to construct arguments, using evidence from multiple sources;
description_plain: Your child can ask questions and use facts from different sources to support their opinions.

subject: Social Studies
description: applying geographic skills to determine and predict patterns and trends of people, places, and events;
description_plain: Your child can use map and location skills to find and predict patterns about people, places, and events.

subject: Social Studies
description: selecting and synthesizing evidence from information sources, including, but not limited to artifacts, primary and secondary sources, charts, graphs, and diagrams in events in world history;
description_plain: Your child can pick important facts from things like artifacts, documents, charts, and pictures to understand world history events.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to the content by
description_plain: Your child will use history and social studies skills to learn about the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining scientific and technological changes and evaluating their impact on American culture, including media.
description_plain: Your child can explain how science and technology changed American life and culture, including media.

subject: Social Studies
description: connecting the legacy of the Civil Rights Movement to the election of Barack Obama; and
description_plain: Your child can connect how the Civil Rights Movement helped lead to Barack Obama being elected.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining social movements, including but not limited to the Vietnam War and the rise of the anti-war movement, Woodstock, the rise of the conservative movement and the election of Ronald Reagan, Women's Movement, Gay Rights Movement, Pro-life Movement, and an increased domestic focus on HIV/AIDS, the rise of antisemitism and hate crimes, and domestic terrorism;
description_plain: Your child can explain important social movements like the Vietnam War protests, Women's Rights, Gay Rights, and others that shaped America.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing key events and conditions that have given rise to terrorism as an attack on democracy and the United States' role in defending democracy, including, but not limited to the 1993 bombing of the World Trade Center, the 2000 bombing of the USS Cole, attacks on U.S. Embassies in Kenya and Tanzania, and the 9/11 attacks on the United States in 2001;
description_plain: Your child can study major terrorist attacks and understand how the U.S. works to protect democracy.

subject: Social Studies
description: assessing the development of and changes in domestic policies due to Supreme Court decisions and acts of Congress, including, but not limited to Brown v. Board of Education, the Federal Highway Act of 1956, the American Indian Movement (AIM), the Indian SelfDetermination and Education Assistance Act, the Equal Pay Act, the Americans with Disabilities Act, Title IX of the Education Amendments of 1972, the Marriage Equality Act, Obergefell v. Hodges, Gideon v. Wainwright, Miranda v. Arizona, and Roe v. Wade leading to Dobbs v. Jackson Women’s Health Organization;
description_plain: Your child can look at important court cases and laws to see how they changed U.S. policies and rights over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to analyze political and social conditions in the United States during the second half of the 20th century and early 21st century by
description_plain: Your child will use history and social studies skills to study political and social changes in the U.S. from the late 1900s to early 2000s.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing the effect of the Black Power Movement.
description_plain: Your child can explain what the Black Power Movement was and its effects.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining how the tenets of the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP), the 1963 March on Washington, the Civil Rights Act of 1964, and the Voting Rights Act of 1965 had an effect on all Americans; and
description_plain: Your child can explain how groups and laws like the NAACP and Civil Rights Acts affected all Americans.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing key events, including, but not limited to the murder of Emmett Till, bus boycotts, Little Rock Central High School desegregation, Greensboro sit-ins, Freedom Rides, Birmingham demonstrations, the 1963 March on Washington, the Freedom Summer, and Selma to Montgomery Marches, with additional emphasis on events in Virginia;
description_plain: Your child can study key Civil Rights events like bus boycotts and sit-ins, especially those in Virginia.

subject: Social Studies
description: evaluating the legacy of Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr., including "A Letter from a Birmingham Jail,” civil disobedience, the Southern Christian Leadership Conference, the “I Have a Dream” speech, and his assassination;
description_plain: Your child can understand Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.'s work, speeches, and his impact on civil rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: evaluating and explaining the impact of the Brown v. Board of Education decision and Virginia’s response of Massive Resistance, including, but not limited to the roles of Barbara Johns, R.R. Moton High School in Prince Edward County, Thurgood Marshall, and Oliver W. Hill, Sr;
description_plain: Your child can explain the Brown v. Board of Education decision and how Virginia responded to school desegregation.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing the origins of the Civil Rights Movement, the effects of segregation, and efforts to desegregate schools, transportation, and public areas;
description_plain: Your child can learn about how the Civil Rights Movement started and efforts to end segregation in schools and public places.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to analyze the causes and effects of the Civil Rights Movement by
description_plain: Your child will use history and social studies skills to understand what caused the Civil Rights Movement and what it changed.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining how American foreign policy pressure, economic power and defense policy, and the assertion of American values led to the end of the Cold War.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the United States used its power, values, and defense strategies to help end the Cold War.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing the changing role of the United States in Asia, including Korea, Vietnam, and China, and the experiences of refugees from those nations; and
description_plain: Your child understands how the U.S. role changed in Asia during the Cold War and learns about the experiences of refugees from Korea, Vietnam, and China.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing events and leaders of the Cold War, including the Bay of Pigs, the Cuban Missile Crisis, John F. Kennedy, and Nikita Khrushchev;
description_plain: Your child can describe important Cold War events and leaders like the Bay of Pigs, Cuban Missile Crisis, John F. Kennedy, and Nikita Khrushchev.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining the long-term impact of the Marshall Plan, the formation of NATO, the Warsaw Pact, and the efforts of the United States to protect Western Europe;
description_plain: Your child explains how the Marshall Plan, NATO, and the Warsaw Pact affected Europe and how the U.S. worked to protect Western Europe.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining the origins and early development of the Cold War and how it changed American foreign policy, including, but not limited to the Truman Doctrine and the policy of containment;
description_plain: Your child understands how the Cold War started and how it changed U.S. foreign policy, including ideas like the Truman Doctrine and containment.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to analyze the United States’ foreign policy during the Cold War era by
description_plain: Your child will use history skills to study how the U.S. handled foreign policy during the Cold War.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the significance of America’s role in the Allied victory, the Marshall Plan, and the significance of the United Nations.
description_plain: Your child can describe why America’s role in winning World War II, the Marshall Plan, and the United Nations was important.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining American military intelligence and technology, including island hopping, the Manhattan Project, and the bombings of the Japanese cities of Hiroshima and Nagasaki; and
description_plain: Your child understands American military intelligence and technology during World War II, including island hopping, the Manhattan Project, and the bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing the Holocaust, beginning with the history and role of antisemitism in the persecution of Jews, the persecutions of other targeted groups, challenges related to the immigration of Jews, Hitler’s “Final Solution,” liberation, post-war trials, post-war immigration to the United States, and the creation of the modern state of Israel;
description_plain: Your child learns about the Holocaust, including the history of antisemitism, persecution of groups, immigration challenges, the Final Solution, liberation, trials, and the creation of Israel.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing major battles of World War II, including Midway, Normandy, Iwo Jima, Okinawa, and the Battle of the Bulge, as well as battles in Holland, Italy, and North Africa;
description_plain: Your child can describe major World War II battles like Midway, Normandy, Iwo Jima, Okinawa, the Battle of the Bulge, and battles in Holland, Italy, and North Africa.

subject: Social Studies
description: evaluating and explaining the contributions of heroic military units including, but not limited to segregated, minority units, women, and the role of Virginia units in the America war effort;
description_plain: Your child explains the important contributions of military units, including minority groups, women, and Virginia units during World War II.

subject: Social Studies
description: identifying the similarities and differences in the strategy, major battles, and impacts of key leaders of the Axis and Allied Powers;
description_plain: Your child identifies similarities and differences in strategies, battles, and leaders of the Axis and Allied Powers in World War II.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing the causes and events that led to America’s involvement, including the attack on Pearl Harbor, the U.S. response with Executive Order 9066 and the incarceration of Japanese Americans, and the Supreme Court case Korematsu v. United States;
description_plain: Your child understands the causes that led the U.S. into World War II, including Pearl Harbor, the response with Executive Order 9066, Japanese American incarceration, and the Korematsu Supreme Court case.

subject: Social Studies
description: comparing and contrasting totalitarianism in Imperial Japan, communist Soviet Union, fascist Italy, and Nazi Germany;
description_plain: Your child compares totalitarian governments in Imperial Japan, the Soviet Union, Italy, and Nazi Germany.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to analyze the United States’ involvement in World War II by
description_plain: Your child will use history skills to study the United States’ involvement in World War II.

subject: Social Studies
description: evaluating and explaining how Franklin D. Roosevelt’s New Deal measures addressed the Great Depression and expanded the government’s role in the economy, its features, and effects.
description_plain: Your child explains how Franklin D. Roosevelt’s New Deal helped the country during the Great Depression and changed the government's role in the economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining the causes of the Great Depression, including, but not limited to bank failures, stock purchases on margins, credit, overproduction, high tariffs and protectionism, and the 1929 stock market crash; and
description_plain: Your child understands the causes of the Great Depression, like bank failures, risky stock buying, too much production, high tariffs, and the 1929 stock market crash.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to describe the effects of the Great Depression and New Deal policies on the United States by
description_plain: Your child will use history skills to learn how the Great Depression and New Deal policies affected the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: examining the Great Migration and its influence on the Harlem Renaissance, prompting new trends in literature, music, and art, and the work of writers, including, but not limited to Zora Neale Hurston and Langston Hughes.
description_plain: Your child learns about the Great Migration and how it influenced the Harlem Renaissance, including new trends in literature, music, art, and writers like Zora Neale Hurston and Langston Hughes.

subject: Social Studies
description: examining the changing role of women in society and in the passage of the Eighteenth Amendment and the Nineteenth Amendment to the Constitution of the United States; and
description_plain: Your child understands how women’s roles changed in society and how they helped pass the 18th and 19th Amendments to the U.S. Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing the Roaring 20s, post-wartime effects on the American economy, how life changed as a result of innovation and inventions, and the diffusion of American popular culture;
description_plain: Your child will learn how the 1920s changed life in America through new inventions, economic growth after World War I, and the spread of popular culture.

subject: Social Studies
description: examining the purposes of Marcus Garvey’s “Back-to-Africa” movement, the American Civil Liberties Union, the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People, and the Anti-Defamation League;
description_plain: Your child will explore why Marcus Garvey wanted African Americans to return to Africa and learn about important groups that worked to protect civil rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing the effects of changes in immigration to the United States including, but not limited to the Immigration Act of 1918 and the Immigration Act of 1924;
description_plain: Your child will understand how new immigration laws in the early 1900s affected who could come to the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing the connections between the Bolshevik Revolution and the First Red Scare, anarchist bombings, and the Palmer Raids;
description_plain: Your child will study how the Russian Revolution led to fear of communism in the U.S. and caused events like bombings and government raids.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing the attacks on civil liberties, including, but not limited to the reemergence of the Ku Klux Klan, the Chicago riot of 1919, the Tulsa Race Massacre and the decimation of Black Wall Street, and the institution of redlining and resulting racial wealth gaps;
description_plain: Your child will learn about attacks on civil rights in the 1920s, including the rise of the Ku Klux Klan, race riots, and unfair housing practices that hurt Black communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to understand key international and domestic events, interests, and philosophies of the 1920s and 1930s by
description_plain: Your child will use history skills to understand important events and ideas in the U.S. and world during the 1920s and 1930s.

subject: Social Studies
description: evaluating and explaining the terms of the Treaty of Versailles, including, but not limited to the national debate in response to the formation of the League of Nations.
description_plain: Your child will explain the Treaty of Versailles and how Americans debated joining the League of Nations after World War I.

subject: Social Studies
description: evaluating the United States’ involvement in World War I, including, but not limited to Woodrow Wilson’s Fourteen Points and the establishment of the League of Nations; and
description_plain: Your child will learn why the U.S. joined World War I and study President Wilson’s plan for peace and the League of Nations.

subject: Social Studies
description: evaluating the events, leaders, and changes that brought America out of a period of isolationism to enter WWI;
description_plain: Your child will explore the events and leaders that moved America from staying out of world conflicts to entering World War I.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining the international significance of U.S. decisions and actions, including, but not limited to the Roosevelt Corollary to the Monroe Doctrine, the Spanish-American War, the acquisition of Alaska and Hawaii, and the Panama Canal construction;
description_plain: Your child will understand how U.S. actions like the Spanish-American War and building the Panama Canal affected the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining changes in foreign policy of the United States toward Latin America and Asia and the growing influence of the United States, including, but not limited to the impact of the Spanish-American War;
description_plain: Your child will learn how the U.S. changed its foreign policy toward Latin America and Asia and became more influential after the Spanish-American War.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to analyze the emerging role of the United States in world affairs during the end of the 19th and early 20th centuries by
description_plain: Your child will use history skills to study how the U.S. started playing a bigger role in world affairs around 1900.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining the emergence of public colleges, HBCUs, and land-grant institutions in Virginia and the United States as a way to expand educational opportunities and build specific skills and knowledge in agricultural and technological advances.
description_plain: Your child will learn about the growth of public colleges, historically Black colleges, and land-grant schools that helped more people get education and skills.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing the effects of prejudice, discrimination, and “Jim Crow” laws including, but not limited to the responses of Booker T. Washington and W.E.B. DuBois, lynching and racial terror, race riots, the suppression of voting rights in Virginia and other Southern states, Ida B. Wells-Barnett’s anti-lynching crusade, the practice of eugenics, and the U.S. Supreme Court 1927 Buck v. Bell decision; and
description_plain: Your child will study how laws and attitudes like Jim Crow affected African Americans and how leaders and activists responded to racism and violence.

subject: Social Studies
description: examining the “Byrd machine” and its dominance in Virginia government in the first half of the 20th century;
description_plain: Your child will learn about the powerful political group called the 'Byrd machine' that controlled Virginia’s government in the early 1900s.

subject: Social Studies
description: evaluating and explaining the Progressive Movement and the impact of its legislation, including, but not limited to regulations for pollution, child labor, and food safety;
description_plain: Your child will explore the Progressive Movement and how new laws helped reduce pollution, child labor, and unsafe food.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining the social and cultural impact of industrialization, including, but not limited to rapid urbanization, the effects on living and working conditions, the development of labor unions, and the emergence of more leisure time and activities;
description_plain: Your child will understand how industrialization changed cities, work, and daily life, including the rise of labor unions and more free time.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing the transformation of the American economy from agrarian to industrial, the growth of cities and trade, the role of the railroads and communication systems, and the concentration of wealth and mass production that created goods at cheaper and faster rates, including, but not limited to industrial leaders such as Andrew Carnegie, Andrew Mellon, and John D. Rockefeller and the growth of American philanthropy;
description_plain: Your child will learn how America’s economy shifted from farming to industry, how cities and trade grew, and how leaders like Carnegie and Rockefeller shaped business and giving.

subject: Social Studies
description: examining and evaluating the motivations, contributions, and challenges immigrants to the United States faced before, during, and upon arrival;
description_plain: Your child will study why immigrants came to the U.S., what they contributed, and the challenges they faced before and after arriving.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing the effects of westward movement and the admission of new states on the Indigenous peoples and the conflicts with the U.S. government, including, but not limited to the Battle of Little Bighorn and Wounded Knee;
description_plain: Your child will learn how westward expansion affected Native American peoples and led to conflicts like the Battle of Little Bighorn and Wounded Knee.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to analyze how the nation grew and changed from the end of Reconstruction through the early 20th century b
description_plain: Your child can look at how the United States grew and changed from the end of Reconstruction through the early 1900s.

subject: Social Studies
description: evaluating the role of the biracial Readjuster Party in Virginia during Reconstruction in providing funds to expand a system of public schools and expanding employment opportunities for African Americans.
description_plain: Your child understands how the biracial Readjuster Party in Virginia helped fund public schools and created jobs for African Americans during Reconstruction.

subject: Social Studies
description: evaluating Virginia’s stance on the Fourteenth Amendment, Virginia’s 1870 Constitution, and readmittance to Congress; and
description_plain: Your child can explain Virginia’s views on the Fourteenth Amendment, its 1870 Constitution, and how it rejoined Congress.

subject: Social Studies
description: evaluating and explaining the political and economic impact of the war and Reconstruction, including the adoption of the Thirteenth, Fourteenth, and Fifteenth Amendments to the Constitution of the United States, sharecropping, the Freedman’s Bureau, and the rise of white supremacist groups;
description_plain: Your child understands how the Civil War and Reconstruction affected politics and the economy, including new amendments, sharecropping, the Freedman’s Bureau, and the rise of white supremacist groups.
