description_plain: Your child understands what the mottos 'E Pluribus Unum' and 'In God We Trust' mean and why they are important in history.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing the five values of liberty, egalitarianism, individualism, populism, and laissezfaire government as described by Alexis de Tocqueville;
description_plain: Your child can explain five key American values: liberty, equality, individualism, populism, and limited government.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to explore and understand the significance, reverence, and pride around the foundation of the American republic by
description_plain: Your child will explore and understand why Americans respect and take pride in the country's founding.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining the amendment process.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Constitution can be changed through amendments.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing how the Bill of Rights affirms natural rights as something that precedes politics; and
description_plain: Your child understands that the Bill of Rights protects natural rights that exist before government.

subject: Social Studies
description: connecting the fundamental principles of checks and balances and separation of powers to the three branches of government;
description_plain: Your child can connect how checks and balances and separation of powers work between the three branches of government.

subject: Social Studies
description: examining the differences between the powers and authority of state and national governments;
description_plain: Your child can explain how state and national governments have different powers and responsibilities.

subject: Social Studies
description: defining the structure and authority of the national government as outlined in Article I, Article II, and Article III;
description_plain: Your child understands how the national government is organized and what powers it has according to the first three articles of the Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: evaluating the purposes for government stated in the Preamble;
description_plain: Your child can explain the reasons for having a government based on the Preamble to the Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: examining the ratification debates and The Federalist Papers, including but not limited to No. 10 and No. 51;
description_plain: Your child can study and explain the debates about approving the Constitution and important essays like The Federalist Papers No. 10 and No. 51.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to analyze the Virginia and United States Constitutions and the Bill of Rights by
description_plain: Your child will use history skills to study and understand the Virginia and U.S. Constitutions and the Bill of Rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: recognizing the freedom of the individual.
description_plain: Your child understands that individuals have personal freedoms that should be respected.

subject: Social Studies
description: recognizing the necessity of compromise; and
description_plain: Your child knows that people need to work together and make compromises to solve problems.

subject: Social Studies
description: recognizing majority rule and minority rights;
description_plain: Your child understands that while most decisions are made by majority vote, the rights of smaller groups must still be protected.

subject: Social Studies
description: recognizing the equality of all citizens under the law;
description_plain: Your child knows that all citizens are equal under the law and should be treated fairly.

subject: Social Studies
description: comparing structures of government including constitutional republic, autocracy, direct democracy, representative democracy, the presidential system, and the parliamentary system;
description_plain: Your child can compare different types of governments, like republics, autocracies, and democracies, and understand how they work.

subject: Social Studies
description: explaining the concepts of popular sovereignty, natural rights, the rule of law, selfgovernment, and “consent of the governed”;
description_plain: Your child can explain important ideas like people having power in government, natural rights, following laws, self-government, and agreeing to be governed.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to describe the concept of democracy by
description_plain: Your child will use history skills to describe what democracy means and how it works.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing George Mason’s Virginia Declaration of Rights, Thomas Jefferson’s Virginia Statute for Religious Freedom, and James Madison’s leadership role in securing adoption of the Bill of Rights by the First Congress.
description_plain: Your child can study important Virginia documents and leaders who helped create the Bill of Rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: evaluating the foundational principles expressed in the Constitution of Virginia, the Declaration of Independence, the Articles of Confederation, and the Constitution of the United States; and
description_plain: Your child can explain the main ideas in important documents like the Virginia Constitution, Declaration of Independence, Articles of Confederation, and U.S. Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyzing the foundational principles found in historic writings and prior governing documents, including the Magna Carta, charters of the Virginia Company of London (April 10, 1606; May 23, 1609; and March 12, 1612), the works of Enlightenment philosophers (Locke, Hobbes, Rousseau, and others), the Great Awakening, and the English Bill of Rights;
description_plain: Your child can study early important writings and ideas from history and philosophy that influenced American government.

subject: Social Studies
description: describing the features of a democratic republic as influenced by forms of Athenian democracy and the Roman Republic;
description_plain: Your child understands how the U.S. democratic republic was influenced by ancient Athens and Rome.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to understand the foundations of American constitutional government by
description_plain: Your child will use history skills to learn about the basic ideas behind American government.

subject: Social Studies
description: contextualizing, corroborating, and evaluating sources for credibility, propaganda, and bias to determine patterns and trends in Virginia and United States government and politics.
description_plain: Your child can check if sources about government are trustworthy and recognize bias or propaganda to understand political patterns.

subject: Social Studies
description: developing products that reflect an understanding of research and content to make real-life connections; and
description_plain: Your child can create projects that show they understand research and connect it to real-life situations.

subject: Social Studies
description: engaging and communicating as an informed individual with persons with different perspectives;
description_plain: Your child can talk and listen respectfully to people who have different opinions.

subject: Social Studies
description: using economic decision-making models to analyze and explain the incentives for and consequences of a specific choice;
description_plain: Your child can use simple economic models to explain why people make certain choices and what happens because of those choices.

subject: Social Studies
description: determining cause and effect to analyze connections;
description_plain: Your child can figure out what causes things to happen and what effects follow.

subject: Social Studies
description: comparing and contrasting historical, cultural, economic, and political perspectives;
description_plain: Your child can compare different views from history, culture, money, and politics to see how they are alike and different.

subject: Social Studies
description: investigating and analyzing evidence from multiple sources to construct arguments and draw conclusions;
description_plain: Your child can look at information from many places to build ideas and make conclusions.

subject: Social Studies
description: questioning and using inquiry to construct arguments, using evidence from multiple sources;
description_plain: Your child can ask questions and use facts from different sources to support their ideas.

subject: Social Studies
description: applying geographic skills to determine and/or predict patterns and trends;
description_plain: Your child can use map and location skills to find and guess patterns and trends.

subject: Social Studies
description: selecting and synthesizing evidence from information sources, including, but not limited to artifacts, primary and secondary sources, charts, graphs, and diagrams, to question and understand government and politics;
description_plain: Your child can pick and combine facts from things like artifacts, documents, charts, and graphs to ask questions and understand government and politics.

subject: Social Studies
description: The student will apply history and social science skills to the content by
description_plain: Your child will use history and social science skills to learn about government and society.
